- DevNexus-generated comments include stable markers or fingerprints to prevent duplicate/echo comments.
- Execution records a sync run summary with created, updated, skipped, conflicted, and blocked counts plus provider links.
- Live external writes require explicit provider write policy and configured auth identity; default tests use mocked providers only.

Dependencies:
- Depends on `dev-nexus:local-47`.
- Composes with `dev-nexus:local-38` publication identity guardrails and `dev-nexus:local-39` meta-project hosting config before live dogfood writes.

Boundaries:
- Do not implement unrestricted two-way sync.
- Do not propagate deletes.
- Do not enable live GitHub sync in the dogfood project without explicit policy approval.

Verification:
- Mocked GitHub/local provider execution tests for idempotent create, update, link, comment marker, skipped capability, and blocked policy cases.
- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus origin/main after verification passes.

items.title: Expose multi-tracker state in agent status and automation context
items.description:
Type: implementation slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK) after tracker bindings and link records are available.
Source PRD: `docs/component-multi-tracker-prd.md`.

Goal: update low-token status, eligible-work, and agent-launch context so agents understand component tracker bindings, defaults, roles, and linked eligible work without reading raw project config.

User stories covered:
- As a coordinator, I can list eligible work without seeing duplicate copies from multiple trackers.
- As a Mac or Windows agent, I can see whether coordination is local, shared, mirrored, or blocked by policy.

Acceptance criteria:
- Project status, automation status, eligible-work summaries, and agent launch context report tracker ids, providers, roles, enabled/default state, and capability summaries per component.
- Eligible work remains selected from the default tracker unless config explicitly says otherwise.
- Linked provider copies are grouped under one logical work item when link records are available.
- Run and target-cycle facts include selected tracker id and logical item id when available.
- Status output includes sync warnings such as stale mirrors or missing coordination tracker capability without blocking unrelated local work.

Dependencies:
- Depends on `dev-nexus:local-44` and `dev-nexus:local-46`.

Boundaries:
- Do not change coordinator work selection semantics beyond reporting and deduping linked views.
- Do not execute sync.

Verification:
- Focused automation status, eligible-work, target-cycle, and agent launch context tests.
- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus origin/main after verification passes.

items.title: Allow coordination tools to target tracker roles
items.description:
Type: implementation slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK) after tracker registry support lands; live external posting remains policy-gated.
Source PRD: `docs/component-multi-tracker-prd.md`.

Goal: let coordination status, handoff, integrate, and request flows use configured tracker roles such as coordination or external_feedback instead of assuming the component default tracker is the only backing store.

User stories covered:
- As a Mac agent, I can read shared handoff state from a GitHub-backed coordination tracker while work selection remains local.
- As an external reviewer, I can respond in a provider-native thread while DevNexus records the neutral effect locally.

Acceptance criteria:
- Coordination configuration can select a tracker by id or role for handoffs and external request records.
- Work selection and canonical item updates still use the component default tracker unless policy says otherwise.
- Handoff records include selected work item, logical item id, tracker references, branch facts, and provider target refs.
- Integration planning can read handoffs from a coordination tracker and work details from the primary/default tracker.
- Provider capability gaps for reading/writing comments are reported clearly.
- Live external posting stays disabled unless coordination provider policy allows it.

Dependencies:
- Depends on `dev-nexus:local-44` and `dev-nexus:local-45`.
- Complements `dev-nexus:local-16` and `dev-nexus:local-18` for shared provider and external posting policy decisions.

Boundaries:
- Do not infer approval or merge permission from mirrored coordination records.
- Do not create Vibe workspaces, sessions, executions, or implementation workers.

Verification:
- Focused coordination status/handoff/integrate/request tests with local and mocked external coordination trackers.
- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus origin/main after verification passes.

items.title: Document multi-tracker configuration and migration guidance
items.description:
Type: documentation and migration slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK) after the core multi-tracker shape stabilizes.
Source PRD: `docs/component-multi-tracker-prd.md`.

Goal: document how users configure multiple component trackers, preserve default tracker compatibility, link existing external items, dry-run sync, and migrate safely from local to shared providers.

User stories covered:
- As a maintainer, I can migrate a component from local work items to GitHub or Jira without losing history.
- As a project maintainer, I can choose which items are mirrored externally and which remain local.

Acceptance criteria:
- README or docs explain tracker bindings, default tracker behavior, roles, link records, sync policy, dry-run planning, and one-way sync limitations.
- Documentation includes examples for local primary plus GitHub mirror/coordination tracker.
- Migration guidance covers linking existing external issues before enabling sync.
- Safety notes cover dry-run-first behavior, no delete propagation, no two-way sync by default, and provider identity guardrails.
- Agent-facing guidance explains how coordinators should treat canonical versus mirrored work items.

Dependencies:
- Depends on the implemented shape from `dev-nexus:local-44` through the relevant sync/coordination slices.

Boundaries:
- Do not document unsupported two-way sync or live external writes as available.

Verification:
- Static documentation review.
- npm run check when feasible if docs are covered by repository checks.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus origin/main after verification passes.

items.title: Choose first live multi-tracker dogfood sync policy
items.description:
Type: human-in-the-loop (HITL) policy decision slice
Readiness: blocked on provider/write policy and dogfood target choice.
Source PRD: `docs/component-multi-tracker-prd.md` open questions.

Goal: decide the first live dogfood sync policy before enabling real external provider mutation for multi-tracker sync.

Decision points:
- Should the first live target be GitHub Issues only, or GitHub Issues plus GitHub Project v2 board status?
- Should the dogfood project keep local as primary until bot identity and meta-project hosting guardrails are complete?
- Which item subset should sync first: `dogfood` ready items, coordination-only handoffs, or a smaller manual allowlist?
- Which comments should mirror externally: all DevNexus progress comments, only handoffs, only high-signal status transitions, or none initially?
- Which host-local auth profile or bot identity is allowed to create/update external issues?
- What quiet-period and no-repeat-ping rules apply to external waiting states?

Acceptance criteria:
- Record the chosen provider, repository/project target, auth profile, allowed operations, synced fields, comment policy, status mapping, and initial item filter.
- Confirm whether live writes are allowed or whether the first rollout remains draft-only.
- Update project config or target-state only after the policy is explicit.
- Leave implementation slices blocked from live external mutation until this decision is recorded.

Dependencies:
- Related to `dev-nexus:local-16`, `dev-nexus:local-18`, `dev-nexus:local-38`, and `dev-nexus:local-39`.

Boundaries:
- Do not perform live external provider writes as part of the decision.
- Do not store secrets in shared project state.

Verification:
- Static policy/config review.
- After approval, one harmless dry-run and then one explicitly approved provider smoke against the chosen test target.

Publication notes:
- Dogfood project-state/policy change only unless implementation changes are required later.

items.title: Add portable project path resolution
items.description:
Type: DevNexus project config/path portability slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK)
Source finding: Mac setup exposed that dev-nexus.project.json currently stores Windows absolute component sourceRoot paths. A shared DevNexus meta project should be portable across Windows, macOS, and Linux without rewriting shared config for each host.

Goal: add a generic portable path model and resolver so shared project config can use one OS-neutral path style while DevNexus code and agent surfaces resolve it to host-local filesystem paths.

Design direction:
- Shared config should prefer portable path expressions, not raw OS absolute paths, for component sourceRoot, worktreesRoot, MCP/generated config paths, runtime state roots, and setup paths.
- Use POSIX-style separators in shared config and resolve them through a central DevNexus path resolver on each host.
- Support stable bases such as projectRoot, projectParent, home, and a configured sourcesRoot/workspacesRoot where appropriate.
- Keep legacy absolute string paths accepted for compatibility, but status/setup checks should flag them as non-portable when they block another OS.
- Host-local overrides may still exist for exceptional layouts, but normal Mac/Windows sharing should work from the same shared path expressions.

Acceptance criteria:
- DevNexus has a typed path expression/resolution service with Windows, macOS, and Linux tests.
- Project config validation accepts portable path expressions or normalized portable strings for component source roots while preserving legacy absolute string compatibility.
- Project/component status, setup plan/check, worktree setup, automation context, MCP config refresh, and verification command cwd resolution use the central resolver instead of ad hoc path.resolve calls where project-config paths are involved.
- Setup assistant recommends portable shared paths for new projects and reports legacy absolute paths as portability warnings or blockers with migration guidance.
- Existing projects with absolute paths continue to work on the original host.
- Tests cover Windows and POSIX conversion, project-relative paths, home/source base paths, legacy absolute paths, and serialized JSON stability.

Boundaries:
- Do not store machine-specific secrets, SSH keys, tokens, or browser auth state in shared config.
- Do not hard-code this dogfood project's Windows or Mac layout into DevNexus core.
- Do not require all existing users to migrate before legacy configs continue working.

Verification:
- Focused path resolver and project config tests.
- Focused setup assistant/status/context tests proving a shared portable config resolves correctly on Windows and macOS fixtures.
- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus origin/main after verification passes.
- This should unblock the full new-machine setup flow tracked by dev-nexus:local-42.

items.title: Move automation agent profiles to portable host-local config
items.description:
Type: DevNexus automation/profile portability slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK)
Source finding: portable component source roots now unblock Mac setup, but this dogfood dev-nexus.project.json still stores Windows-specific Codex executable paths and --add-dir paths inside automation.agent.profiles. A Mac can run setup and component work manually, but DevNexus-managed automation launch from shared config remains host-specific.

Goal: separate portable automation profile intent from host-local executable/path material so the same shared meta project can launch coordinators on Windows or macOS without rewriting dev-nexus.project.json.

Acceptance criteria:
- Automation agent profiles can reference a host-local profile id or resolve command/path expressions through the central path resolver.
- Shared config no longer needs raw Windows Codex executable paths or component absolute --add-dir values.
- Host-local profile configuration stores executable paths, app-server endpoints, and OS-specific args outside shared project state.
- Agent launch context reports which host-local profile was selected and blocks clearly if it is missing.
- Existing Windows dogfood launch profile remains compatible through migration or host-local override.
- Tests cover Windows and macOS fixture profile resolution, missing profile blockers, and serialized shared config stability.

Boundaries:
- Do not store tokens, private keys, or browser auth state in shared config.
- Do not make DevNexus choose work; this only prepares the launcher infrastructure.

Verification:
- Focused automation agent profile/launch tests.
- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus origin/main after verification passes.

items.title: Add extensible agent-provider MCP target support
items.description:
Type: agent-provider MCP projection slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK) with current provider docs; no live credentials required.
Source finding: Mac onboarding exposed that DevNexus setup guidance must not assume Codex Desktop. Users may prefer OpenCode or another MCP-capable agent provider with a different project/session and config model.

Goal: make DevNexus agent-provider setup extensible beyond the current Codex/Claude concrete projection writers, starting with OpenCode if its documented MCP config model is stable enough.

Acceptance criteria:
- Agent MCP target configuration distinguishes provider identity, config path, config format/schema, project/session activation notes, and approval/tool-trust semantics where supported.
- `project mcp refresh` reports unsupported provider/config combinations as explicit capability gaps instead of Codex-specific assumptions or generic crashes.
- OpenCode support is implemented when official docs confirm the config shape; otherwise a documented custom-provider/manual-config path exists.
- Setup plan/check output remains provider-neutral and lists configured target agents plus exact generated or manual config files.
- Tests cover Codex, Claude, OpenCode or custom provider fixtures, unsupported provider warnings, and project/session setup-record readiness.

Boundaries:
- Do not edit provider global state, app databases, credentials, or private auth material.
- Do not assume every provider uses Codex TOML or Claude-style JSON.
- Keep DevNexus generic; provider-specific behavior belongs in target adapters.

Verification:
- Focused agent MCP config and setup assistant tests.
- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus origin/main after verification passes.

items.title: Expose referenced planning docs in worker context
items.description:
Type: worker-context setup defect
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK)
Source: during the 2026-05-17 parallel dogfood wave, the local-44 worker prompt referenced `docs/component-multi-tracker-prd.md` inside the prepared component worktree, but DevNexus worktree preparation only projected core context files and did not make referenced root docs available in the worktree. The worker had to report an input gap and continue from acceptance criteria until the coordinator sent the root PRD path manually.

Goal: make prepared worker contexts expose or resolve work-item referenced planning documents so agents do not receive nonexistent doc paths.

Acceptance criteria:
- Worktree preparation detects root-relative doc references from work-item descriptions and/or selected project docs and records them in `.dev-nexus/context/context.json` plus `briefing.md` as read-only inputs with absolute paths.
- Coordinator/worker briefing avoids implying that root docs exist inside the component worktree unless they are explicitly projected there.
- If a referenced planning file is missing, setup reports a clear capability/input gap before launch.
- Tests cover a work item referencing `docs/component-multi-tracker-prd.md` and prove the worker context includes the correct read-only root path.

Boundaries:
- Do not copy arbitrary project docs into source worktrees by default if read-only absolute references are enough.
- Preserve component worktree ownership: root docs are context, not worker write scope.

Verification:
- Focused worker context/worktree prepare tests.
- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus origin/main after verification passes.

items.title: Add active MCP visibility diagnostics for agent sessions
items.description:
Type: DevNexus setup/MCP diagnostics defect
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK)
Source: after Codex was restarted on 2026-05-17, `.codex/config.toml` and `codex mcp list` showed `dev_nexus` and `dev_nexus_pharo` enabled, and direct stdio probes proved both servers initialize and list tools. However the active Codex tool surface in this thread still exposes PLexus/Pharo namespaces but not `mcp__dev_nexus` or `mcp__dev_nexus_pharo`. Setup check only reports a generic provider-session warning, so a coordinator can believe the root is ready while silently falling back to CLI.

Goal: make DevNexus onboarding/setup distinguish generated MCP config, server startability, provider config listing, and active-session tool visibility with actionable diagnostics.

Acceptance criteria:
- Setup check or a provider diagnostics command probes configured stdio/HTTP MCP servers for initialize/tools-list without requiring implementation work.
- For Codex, diagnostics can compare generated config, `codex mcp list` or configured CLI equivalent, and a recorded active-session confirmation step without relying on the fragile plain `codex` app alias.
- The check reports which expected namespaces/tools are configured, startable, provider-listed, and still unconfirmed in the active session.
- Guidance tells the user or coordinator exactly whether to restart/reopen the provider session, record the session, refresh MCP config, or fix a broken command.
- Tests cover configured-but-not-visible, command-not-startable, HTTP endpoint-unreachable, and all-good cases with mocked providers.

Evidence from this run:
- `.codex/config.toml` declares `dev_nexus`, `dev_nexus_pharo`, `plexus_project`, `pharo_launcher`, and `pharo`.
- Exact Codex CLI path can list MCP servers, but plain `codex mcp list` hits an access-denied app alias in this shell.
- Direct stdio probes report `dev-nexus@0.1.0` with 24 tools and `dev-nexus-pharo@0.1.0` with 25 tools.
- Active thread tools still lack the generic DevNexus MCP namespaces.

Verification:
- Focused setup/provider diagnostics tests.
- npm run check.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus origin/main after verification passes.

items.title: Harden npm dogfood package propagation and runtime installs
items.description:
Type: setup/package-install reliability defect
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK)
Source: several dogfood setup and worker runs have depended on freshly published npm dogfood prereleases. Registry propagation or transient fetch failures caused confusing manual recovery: DevNexus-Pharo name-based install initially saw a missing packument after publish, a PLexus worker failed to fetch `@evref-bl/pharo-launcher-mcp` and left partial `node_modules`, and DevNexus alpha.9 install had to be retried after the dogfood tag became visible.

Goal: make DevNexus-owned setup/runtime package installation resilient and explicit so workers do not discover npm propagation or partial-install failures mid-task.

Acceptance criteria:
- DevNexus setup/runtime package installation and publish-smoke guidance use bounded retry/backoff for npm packument/dist-tag visibility after a publish.
- Runtime package install preflight detects partial or stale `node_modules` state and either repairs it through an approved setup command or reports a clear blocker before launching workers.
- Worker context explains that package installation/fetch is setup-owned; workers should not run ad hoc npm install/npx fetches when dependencies are unavailable.
- Failure summaries distinguish registry propagation delay, network failure, missing package/version, and damaged local install state.
- Tests mock delayed npm visibility and partial runtime install directories without live registry access.

Verification:
- Focused setup/runtime package tests.
- npm run check.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus origin/main after verification passes.

items.title: Make local work-item store writes concurrency-safe
items.description:
Type: local work-tracker data-integrity defect
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK)
Source: During the 2026-05-17 PLexus parallel worktree wave, parallel DevNexus local-provider mutations corrupted .dev-nexus/work-items/plexus.json. Two coordination/comment writes reused comment ids and the file ended with a duplicated JSON tail after the closing brace. Subsequent work-item updates and coordination integrate failed with: Unexpected non-whitespace character after JSON at position 72490. The coordinator repaired the file manually by removing only the duplicate suffix.

Goal:
Make local work-item stores safe under concurrent create, update, comment, and coordination handoff operations.

Acceptance criteria:
- Every local-provider mutation uses a per-store transactional write path: acquire lock or equivalent serialization, read fresh state, allocate ids under that lock, write to a temporary file, and atomically replace the store.
- Concurrent comments or updates against the same store produce distinct ids and preserve all records.
- DevNexus never appends or partially rewrites a store that fails JSON parsing.
- Parse failures include the store path, operation, and recovery guidance, without losing the original error detail.
- Tests simulate concurrent create/comment/update/handoff operations against the same local store and assert valid JSON plus preserved records.

Verification:
Run focused local work-tracker provider tests, coordination handoff tests that use the local provider, and npm run check.

items.title: Normalize component-qualified ids in coordination and worktree tools
items.description:
Type: component work-item routing defect
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK)
Source: During the 2026-05-17 PLexus parallel wave, coordination handoff failed for component-qualified ids such as plexus:local-15 even when --component plexus was provided: Local work item not found. The same command succeeded only after switching to provider-local id local-15. DevNexus worktree prepare also accepted the component-qualified work item but produced worker context with a null work-item title.

Goal:
Make all DevNexus coordination and worktree tools route component-qualified work item ids consistently with work-item get/update/comment.

Acceptance criteria:
- coordination status, handoff, integrate, and request accept component-qualified ids with or without an explicit --component when the component can be resolved safely.
- worktree prepare resolves component-qualified work items to the owning component and includes the work-item title in generated context/briefing metadata.
- Error messages name the requested component, provider-local id, and configured tracker when lookup fails.
- Tests cover local provider items, component-qualified ids, provider-local ids with --component, and ambiguous/mismatched component errors.

Verification:
Run focused coordination CLI/MCP tests, worktree prepare tests, and npm run check.

items.title: Report coordination integration tracker parse failures actionably
items.description:
Type: coordination diagnostics defect
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK), after or alongside the local-provider concurrency fix
Source: During the 2026-05-17 PLexus integration, coordination integrate failed with a raw JSON.parse message after the local tracker store was corrupted. The command did not identify the store path, affected component, failing provider operation, or a safe recovery path.

Goal:
Make coordination integrate and related coordination readers degrade cleanly when tracker stores or handoff comments are malformed.

Acceptance criteria:
- coordination integrate reports component id, tracker id/provider, store path when local, and the failing read/parse stage for malformed tracker state.
- The command returns structured JSON with ok: false and actionable diagnostics instead of crashing with an uncaught parser message.
- Malformed handoff comments are skipped or reported individually without hiding valid handoffs from other work items.
- Tests cover malformed local stores, malformed handoff comment JSON blocks, and valid handoffs coexisting with bad records.

Verification:
Run focused coordination integrate diagnostics tests and npm run check.

items.title: Resolve Windows Codex coordinator command without host-specific shared config
items.description:
Type: automation infrastructure repair
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK)
Source: heartbeat coordinator-loop target cycle target-cycle-coordinator-loop-20260517-t205231-721-z-1 failed before work selection because the configured Codex coordinator profile used bare `codex`, which Windows resolved to the WindowsApps alias and failed with `Acc?s refus?`; the user-local Codex Desktop binary runs correctly.

Goal: make shared DevNexus Codex coordinator profiles portable while avoiding Windows App Execution Alias failures.

Acceptance criteria:
- Shared project profiles can keep `command: codex` without encoding one machine's absolute path.
- On Windows, DevNexus resolves a Codex executor profile's bare `codex` command to the user-local Codex Desktop CLI when it exists, or uses an explicit host-local override when configured.
- The generated shell command quotes resolved command paths safely.
- Tests cover Windows local Codex command resolution and command-path quoting without requiring a real Codex install.
- The dogfood project runtime package is refreshed so `automation coordinator-loop` no longer fails with `Acc?s refus?` before coordinator selection.

Verification:
- Run focused agent profile command-resolution tests.
- Run `npm run check` in DevNexus when feasible.
- Smoke the dogfood coordinator launch path with a harmless command/profile check or a bounded coordinator-loop dry path that does not start unrelated implementation.

Publication notes:
- Publish DevNexus to origin/main and npm dogfood tag if verification passes, then refresh `.dev-nexus/runtime/npm-tools` in the dogfood root.

items.title: Make DevNexus-launched Git commands noninteractive
items.description:
Type: automation ergonomics defect
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK)
Source: During a dogfood meta-repo rebase on 2026-05-17, `git rebase --continue` opened VS Code on `.git/COMMIT_EDITMSG` because the host global Git `core.editor` is VS Code with `--wait`. Agents should not rely on the human to close editors or complete interactive Git prompts.

Goal: make DevNexus-launched commands noninteractive for Git editor prompts by default.

Acceptance criteria:
- Agent launch environments set `GIT_EDITOR=true`, `GIT_SEQUENCE_EDITOR=true`, and `GIT_MERGE_AUTOEDIT=no` unless the caller already supplied explicit values.
- Command-executor environments use the same noninteractive Git defaults.
- Tests prove existing caller-provided values are preserved and defaults are added when absent.
- The change does not alter the user's global or repository Git configuration.

Verification:
- Focused automation environment tests.
- `npm run check`.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus origin/main after verification passes; publish dogfood prerelease if needed by automation runtime.

items.title: Stop target-report relaunch after completed cycle closes all eligible work
items.description: Type: automation target-report defect. Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK). Evidence: after coordinator-loop coordinator-loop-20260517-t220947-388-z-1 closed all six selected dogfood work items, eligible-work returned zero but target-report still returned relaunch because the latest completed managed cycle retained the pre-run eligible count of 6. Goal: target-report should stop when a completed coordinator-loop leaves no eligible work. Acceptance: add post-run/no-work facts or current eligible reconciliation, tests for all-closed completion returning stop, preserve relaunch for failed/blocked/still-eligible cycles. Verification: focused target-report/coordinator-loop tests and npm run check. Publication: direct DevNexus integration and dogfood prerelease if runtime needs it.

items.title: Harden coordinator completion status reconciliation
items.description:
Type: automation data-integrity defect
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK)
Source: heartbeat reflection after coordinator-loop-20260518-t054231-514-z-1. The coordinator result file, target-state, and work-item comments recorded dev-nexus:local-50 as completed and published, but the local tracker item remained in_progress until post-cycle reflection reconciled it manually. Eligible-work reported idle only because the item was no longer ready, which could hide a stale in-progress item.

Goal: harden coordinator completion/status reconciliation so DevNexus detects or repairs mismatches between coordinator results, target-cycle facts, and component work-item status.

Acceptance criteria:
- Coordinator finalization validates selected work-item statuses against the result contract and target-cycle completion facts before reporting a clean completed run.
- If a result says a selected item completed but the tracker remains in_progress or ready, DevNexus either updates it through the work-item service or records a structured reconciliation blocker with the component, tracker, and item id.
- Multi-item runs distinguish completed, blocked, failed, and skipped items without forcing every selected item to done.
- Target-report and eligible-work summaries surface stale in_progress coordinator-owned items instead of silently treating them as no-work.
- Tests cover completed single-item, partial multi-item, blocked, failed, and tracker-update failure paths.

Boundaries:
- Do not infer implementation success without a valid coordinator result contract.
- Do not perform live external tracker writes; use local and mocked providers.

Verification:
- Focused coordinator-loop, target-report, and work-item service tests.
- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus origin/main after verification passes; refresh dogfood runtime package if the coordinator-loop runtime needs the fix.

items.title: Stream coordinator-loop progress feedback
items.description:
Type: dogfood UX/infrastructure improvement.
Source: user observed that running 'dev-nexus automation coordinator-loop . --max-ticks 1 --max-runs 1 --json' gives no visible feedback in the Codex app while the nested coordinator is running, making it look stuck.

Problem: --json suppresses tick output until the end, and non-json tick output is only emitted after a coordinator launch finishes. Long nested Codex runs therefore have no lightweight heartbeat/progress signal in the invoking chat or terminal.

Acceptance criteria:
- Add a low-overhead coordinator-loop progress surface that can be consumed while a run is active.
- Preserve final JSON compatibility for automation callers.
- Support at least one machine-readable streaming mode, preferably JSON Lines events on stderr or stdout with a documented flag.
- Emit meaningful events at coordinator-loop start, decision, launch dispatched, run started, run completed/blocked/failed, and final stop.
- Avoid forwarding high-volume nested Codex output by default; progress events should be small and throttled or event-based.
- Document the recommended command for Codex chat use and for automation use.

Verification:
- Add focused tests for streaming/progress behavior and final JSON compatibility.
- Run relevant CLI/coordinator-loop tests and npm run check.

Publication: direct integration to DevNexus origin/main after verification.

items.title: Harden target-cycle record duplicate id behavior
items.description:
Type: implementation hardening slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK)
Source observation: during dev-nexus:local-66 bookkeeping, `automation target-cycle record` accepted a repeated `--cycle-id` and appended a duplicate fact. Historical duplicate ids already exist in `.dev-nexus/automation/target-cycles.json`, so consumers cannot treat the ledger id as unique today.

Policy decision for this slice: reject duplicate `target-cycle record --cycle-id` calls with an actionable, non-interactive recovery message. Do not silently append, upsert, or replace existing records in this first hardening pass.

Goal: make target-cycle fact recording reject duplicate ids so automated coordination cannot silently create ambiguous facts.

Acceptance criteria:
- Repeated `target-cycle record --cycle-id` calls fail before writing another record when the id already exists.
- The error explains how to recover without opening an editor or requiring interactive repair, such as choosing a new id or inspecting the existing record.
- Tests cover duplicate-id rejection for the CLI/service path.
- Coordinator-loop generated records remain unaffected and keep stable run/cycle references.
- Command help or relevant docs state that explicit cycle ids must be unique.

Boundaries:
- Do not rewrite historical ignored local ledgers as part of the source change.
- Do not add upsert/replacement behavior in this slice.
- Do not change target-report semantics beyond what is required to consume the clarified ledger behavior.

Verification:
- Focused target-cycle CLI/service tests.
- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus origin/main after verification passes.

items.title: Clean dogfood project legacy artifacts
items.description:
Type: dogfood project cleanup
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK)
Source: user requested a thorough cleanup of the clean dogfood project; current audit found stale generated worktrees, an old `.mcp.json` pointing at `dev-nexus-control`, tracked overnight live runtime profile notes with old source paths, and target/context files carrying completed-cycle detail as current state.

Goal: remove or shrink legacy dogfood project artifacts that are no longer useful while preserving source commits, active configuration, and current work-item intent.

Acceptance criteria:
- Remove ignored local artifacts that are clearly stale or superseded, including orphaned old-name worktree directories and obsolete `.mcp.json` provider config.
- Remove clean generated dogfood worktree directories only after verifying no tracked changes; leave Git branches/commits intact unless separately proven safe.
- Remove or retire tracked runtime approval notes that should not be reused as current policy.
- Compact target/context documentation so it reflects current state and points to durable work items instead of stale completed-cycle detail.
- Preserve required generated skills/MCP config/runtime package tools that active setup still uses.
- Record follow-up items for cleanup that is outside this repo or requires a migration decision, such as replacing source-root junctions that still point to older checkout locations.

Boundaries:
- Do not delete component source roots or unmerged source branches.
- Do not remove active `.codex/config.toml`, projected skills, or `.dev-nexus/runtime/npm-tools` while MCP config depends on them.
- Do not run live Pharo/PLexus/Docker/runtime cleanup.

Verification:
- Git status clean for dogfood root after tracked cleanup commit.
- DevNexus eligible-work/status still works.
- Source component status remains clean.

Publication notes:
- Commit and push dogfood meta-project state only.

items.title: Umbrella: Make project-local component clones the default layout
items.description:
Type: umbrella / setup design and dogfood migration
Readiness: todo; do not assign this umbrella directly for implementation. Use the sliced child work items below.
Source: dogfood cleanup audit found that this Windows host resolves several component source roots through `C:\dev\code\sources\...` junctions pointing to older external checkout locations. User decision: a DevNexus project should normally clone its component sources inside the project root so project work does not overlap unrelated host activity.

Goal: make project-local component clones the default DevNexus project layout, then migrate this dogfood project to that model.

Default layout decision:
```text
my-dev-nexus-project/
  dev-nexus.project.json
  AGENTS.md
  CONTEXT.md
  PLAN.md
  .agents/
  .dev-nexus/
    work-items/
    automation/
    skills/
    runtime/
  components/
    <component-id>/        # stable component source checkouts
  worktrees/
    <component-id>/        # generated isolated implementation worktrees
```

Design direction:
- Use `components/<component-id>` for normal stable source checkouts, not `git/` and not `.dev-nexus/git/`.
- Keep `.dev-nexus/` for project metadata, generated setup, runtime state, skills, ledgers, and internal caches rather than primary editable source clones.
- Keep generated implementation worktrees under `worktrees/<component-id>/<work-item-or-run>`.
- Support external source roots only through explicit advanced configuration or host-local overrides, not as the default shared project model.
- Setup/preflight should warn when a source root points outside the DevNexus project unless that escape hatch is explicit.

Sliced work items:
- `dev-nexus:local-71` ready: add `componentsRoot:` portable path base and preferred docs/examples for project-local component clones.
- `dev-nexus:local-72` todo/dependent: make `join-existing-project` prepare and verify project-local component checkouts by default.
- `dev-nexus:local-73` todo/dependent: classify source-root topology in setup diagnostics, including external roots and symlink/junction escapes.
- `dev-nexus:local-74` todo/dependent: document project-local component layout and onboarding defaults after behavior lands.
- `dev-nexus:local-75` todo/HITL/dependent: migrate this dogfood project to project-local `components/` checkouts after the setup behavior is published.

Completion criteria for this umbrella:
- The default DevNexus setup/onboarding path creates or verifies component source roots under `components/<component-id>`.
- Shared config can express project-local components without absolute host paths.
- Setup/preflight can distinguish project-local clones, advanced external roots, missing clones, incompatible paths, and suspicious symlink/junction escapes.
- This dogfood project is migrated to project-local component clones and verified on Windows, with a Mac verification handoff.
- Old external checkout locations are left untouched until the user explicitly approves cleanup.

Boundaries:
- Do not delete old checkout locations from this umbrella or its source/setup slices.
- Do not mutate the MCP-Pharo Pharo image Iceberg checkout as part of migration; create a normal project-local Git clone instead.
- Do not store absolute host paths in shared project config.
- Do not put primary source checkouts under `.dev-nexus/`.

items.title: Remove personal bot alias examples from setup output
items.description: Reusable DevNexus setup source and tests should use generic automation identity examples instead of Gabriel-specific Gabot/gabot names. Keep real dogfood project bot aliases configurable in project metadata.

items.title: Add componentsRoot portable path base for project-local component clones
items.description:
Type: implementation slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK)
Parent: dev-nexus:local-69
Source: dogfood source-root cleanup decision. No external PRD is required; this work-item description is the contract.

Goal: add a first-class project-local component path base so shared DevNexus config can express stable source checkouts under the meta project root without absolute host paths or sibling-directory guessing.

Acceptance criteria:
- The central path resolver accepts `componentsRoot:<component-id-or-path>` and resolves it to `<projectRoot>/components/<component-id-or-path>` on Windows, macOS, and Linux path styles.
- Existing project-relative paths, `projectRoot:`, `projectParent:`, `sourcesRoot:`, and compatible absolute legacy paths continue to work.
- Agent profile/path-expression detection and setup/status surfaces that validate portable paths recognize `componentsRoot:` as portable.
- User docs and examples state that `componentsRoot:<component-id>` is the preferred sourceRoot for normal project-local component clones; `sourcesRoot:` remains an advanced external-layout option.
- Focused tests cover Windows and POSIX resolution, empty/tail normalization, compatibility with existing bases, and setup/status path analysis where relevant.

Boundaries:
- Do not migrate this dogfood project's component source roots in this slice.
- Do not remove `sourcesRoot:` compatibility.
- Do not introduce host-local secrets or absolute paths into shared config.

Verification:
- Focused path resolver and setup/status tests.
- `npm run check` when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus origin/main after verification passes.

items.title: Make join-existing-project prepare project-local component checkouts
items.description: Type: implementation slice. Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK) after dev-nexus:local-71 merged into DevNexus main at b4aa3af. Parent: dev-nexus:local-69. Goal: make join-existing-project guide and verify project-local component checkouts under components/<component-id> by default, instead of sending users to sibling sources directories unless the project explicitly chooses that layout. Acceptance: setup plan text and clone commands prefer componentsRoot:<component-id> or resolved <projectRoot>/components/<component-id>; missing clones provide actionable clone/fetch commands using remoteUrl/defaultBranch; dirty checkouts are detected before fetch/pull guidance; incompatible or absent legacy sourceRoot values fall back to project-local components/<component-id>; Windows and macOS fixtures cover fresh meta clones, missing directories, clean checkouts, and dirty blockers. Boundaries: do not auto-delete, move, or overwrite existing checkouts; do not run live provider writes, package installs, Pharo images, Docker, or PLexus runtime services; keep this generic in DevNexus core. Verification: focused setup assistant tests; npm run check when feasible. Publication: direct integration to DevNexus origin/main after verification passes.

items.title: Classify component source-root topology in setup diagnostics
items.description:
Type: implementation/design slice
Readiness: todo/dependent on dev-nexus:local-71 and dev-nexus:local-72
Parent: dev-nexus:local-69
Source: dogfood source-root cleanup decision. No external PRD is required; this work-item description is the contract.

Goal: make setup/preflight explicitly classify component source-root topology so agents can tell project-local clones from intentional external checkouts, missing clones, and accidental symlink/junction escapes.

Acceptance criteria:
- Setup check reports whether each component source root is project-local, explicit external/advanced, legacy external, missing, or incompatible with the current host platform.
- Realpath/symlink/junction analysis warns when a configured project-local-looking path resolves outside the DevNexus project root, with Windows junction and POSIX symlink fixture coverage where feasible.
- The warning language distinguishes intentional external layouts from accidental old checkout reuse and points to the project-local migration path.
- If a host-local override mechanism already exists, setup check recognizes it; if not, this item documents the minimal config shape needed and creates a follow-up rather than baking machine-specific paths into shared config.
- Project/status or setup JSON includes enough structured fields for agents to make non-interactive decisions without scraping prose.

Boundaries:
- Do not delete or rewrite external source roots.
- Do not require all existing projects to migrate immediately.
- Do not store absolute host paths in shared project config as the normal solution.

Verification:
- Focused setup/status tests for project-local, `sourcesRoot:`, absolute, missing, junction/symlink, and incompatible path cases.
- `npm run check` when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus origin/main after verification passes.

items.title: Document project-local component layout and onboarding defaults
items.description:
Type: documentation/onboarding slice
Readiness: todo/dependent on dev-nexus:local-71 and dev-nexus:local-72
Parent: dev-nexus:local-69
Source: dogfood source-root cleanup decision. No external PRD is required; this work-item description is the contract.

Goal: update user-facing DevNexus setup guidance so new users and new machines understand the project-local component layout as the default and know when external roots are an advanced exception.

Acceptance criteria:
- Getting-started and setup docs show the preferred layout with `components/<component-id>` stable source checkouts and `worktrees/<component-id>/<run-or-item>` generated implementation worktrees.
- The docs explain why `.dev-nexus/` is internal support state and should not contain primary editable component clones.
- Mac/Windows onboarding examples use a fresh meta project root and project-local component clones without sibling-directory guessing.
- External roots are documented as explicit advanced overrides, with warnings about overlap with unrelated host activity.
- Agent-facing guidance tells workers to treat generated worktrees as writable implementation checkouts and stable `components/` clones as canonical component source roots.

Boundaries:
- Do not document behavior as available until corresponding setup/path support exists.
- Do not include this dogfood host's absolute paths as generic examples.

Verification:
- Static documentation review.
- `npm run check` if docs are included in repository checks.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus origin/main after verification passes.

items.title: Migrate dogfood sources to project-local components layout
items.description:
Type: dogfood migration slice
Readiness: todo/HITL after dev-nexus:local-71, dev-nexus:local-72, and dev-nexus:local-73 are completed and published
Parent: dev-nexus:local-69
Source: dogfood source-root cleanup decision. No external PRD is required; this work-item description is the contract.

Goal: migrate this clean dogfood meta project from external `sourcesRoot:` junction-backed component roots to project-local component clones under `components/<component-id>` once DevNexus setup support is ready.

Acceptance criteria:
- Clone or otherwise prepare the three configured components under this meta project root: DevNexus, DevNexus-Pharo, and DevNexus-TypeScript.
- Preserve each component's remote, default branch, unpushed work, and dirty-state safety; stop and report if a component has unresolved local work that would be lost or ambiguous.
- Update shared dogfood config to use `componentsRoot:<component-id>` or the accepted project-local expression for each component sourceRoot.
- Refresh DevNexus MCP/skills/config as needed after the config migration.
- Run setup check on Windows and, if available, provide the exact Mac check command for the Mac agent to run against the same shared config.
- Verify `git status --short --branch` for every project-local component checkout and the meta project.
- Leave old external checkout locations untouched until the user separately approves cleanup.

Boundaries:
- Do not delete old `C:\dev\code\sources` junction targets or older checkouts in this slice.
- Do not migrate PLexus, pharo-launcher-mcp, or MCP-Pharo here; they are not configured components of this dogfood project.
- Do not store host-specific absolute paths in shared config.

Verification:
- `dev-nexus setup check . join-existing-project --platform windows --json` passes or reports only expected active-session warnings.
- `dev-nexus project status . --json` and `dev-nexus automation eligible-work . --json` work after migration.
- Component Git status summary is recorded in the work item.

Publication notes:
- Commit and push dogfood meta-project config/state changes through the configured bot remote after migration succeeds.

items.title: Stabilize full-suite coordination test timeout
items.description: Default npm run check repeatedly times out in nexusCoordination clean-handoff test under full-suite load; isolated test and serialized full suite pass. Make default check deterministic without weakening coverage.

items.title: Slice remote host execution PRD into work items
items.description:
Type: planning slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK)
Source plan: docs/remote-host-execution-prd.md.

Goal: turn the remote host execution PRD into component-owned implementation work items for generic DevNexus host/runner support and plugin-specific capability probes.

Acceptance criteria:
- Slice the PRD into bounded DevNexus core, DevNexus-Pharo, PLexus, and project-state work items where appropriate.
- Keep the durable coordination layer separate from the SSH/Tailscale execution transport.
- First slices must be read-only or verification-only by default; live runtime, package install, Docker, GUI, and destructive cleanup profiles remain human-in-the-loop.
- Include identity policy acceptance criteria so agent-created Git/GitHub activity cannot fall back to the human account.
- Record sequencing, blockers, and verification guidance on each created item.

Verification:
- List created work items through the DevNexus work-item CLI.
- Confirm no live network, Tailscale, SSH command execution, Pharo image startup, Docker, package install, or destructive cleanup is required for the slicing pass.

Publication notes:
- Dogfood planning state stays in the meta project until the sliced source work lands through each component publication policy.

items.title: Model coordination roles for merge and approval authority
items.description: Type: coordination/publication policy slice. Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK). Source: user clarified that integration behavior is a coordination role decision: an agent may be a maintainer that can merge verified work, a contributor that opens PRs and waits for approval, a reviewer that can approve but not merge, or an observer that only reports. Goal: add explicit project/component/provider role policy for agent authority instead of encoding this only as informal target-state text. Acceptance: config can declare role/authority for automation actors per component or provider; agent context/status reports whether the current actor may push, open PRs, approve, merge, request review, or only hand off; publication/coordination commands block when the role does not allow the requested action; GitHub PR and direct-push examples are documented without hard-coding dogfood account names; tests cover maintainer direct merge, contributor PR-only, reviewer approval-only, and observer read-only behavior with mocked providers. Boundaries: do not make DevNexus choose implementation work; do not infer approval from assignment or comments; do not require live GitHub credentials. Dependencies: builds on dev-nexus:local-38 actor/remote guardrails and dev-nexus:local-50 coordination tracker roles. Verification: focused config/status/publication policy tests and npm run check when feasible. Publication: direct integration to DevNexus main after verification.

items.title: Add remote host registry and host-local overlay model
items.description:
Type: remote host execution tracer-bullet implementation slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK)
Source PRD: docs/remote-host-execution-prd.md
Parent: dev-nexus:local-77

Goal: add the first generic DevNexus host registry model so shared project state can name trusted hosts and required capabilities while host-local overlays keep transport details, paths, and credentials out of the meta project.

User stories covered:
- As a coordinator, I can see which logical hosts exist and what capabilities they advertise before requesting platform-specific verification.
- As a maintainer, I can keep Tailscale addresses, SSH users, command paths, and credential profile details in host-local state rather than shared config.

Acceptance criteria:
- Project config can declare logical host ids, display names, platform/capability tags, enabled state, and optional notes without storing secrets or machine-local paths.
- Host-local DevNexus home config can provide transport details and workspace roots for a declared host by stable host id.
- Project/status or a new low-token status surface reports host ids, capability tags, whether host-local overlay details are configured, and any missing overlay warnings.
- Setup check warns when shared project config contains forbidden host-local details such as Tailscale IPs, SSH usernames, credential material, absolute personal paths, live ports, or runtime artifact paths.
- Existing projects without host registry config continue to load with an empty host list.

Boundaries:
- Do not run SSH, Tailscale, remote commands, package installs, live Pharo images, Docker, or host process cleanup in this slice.
- Do not make DevNexus core understand Pharo Launcher, PLexus, or image-side MCP capabilities beyond generic capability strings.

Verification:
- Focused config normalization/status/setup tests for no hosts, shared host declarations, host-local overlays, missing overlays, and forbidden shared fields.
- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus main through the configured automation account after verification passes.

items.title: Add runner profile schema with safety classes and approval gates
items.description:
Type: remote host execution implementation slice
Readiness: todo/dependent on dev-nexus:local-79
Source PRD: docs/remote-host-execution-prd.md
Parent: dev-nexus:local-77

Goal: model generic runner profiles that constrain what a local or remote host may execute for a DevNexus project.

User stories covered:
- As a human, I can approve one live-runtime runner profile without granting broad arbitrary shell access.
- As a maintainer, I can see whether a requested remote action is read-only, project-local mutation, live-runtime, or destructive before it runs.

Acceptance criteria:
- Project config can declare runner profiles with required capabilities, allowed operation classes, command profile references, timeout/output limits, artifact retention policy, credential identity policy, mutation class, and approval requirement.
- Validation rejects runner profiles that allow live-runtime or destructive work without explicit approval metadata or policy gates.
- Status surfaces show each profile's required capabilities, mutation class, approval state, and missing host capability warnings.
- Agent-facing context summarizes runner profile policy without exposing host-local secrets, paths, SSH targets, or tokens.
- Tests cover read-only, verification-only, project-local mutation, live-runtime, destructive, and invalid profile combinations.

Boundaries:
- Do not execute runner profiles in this slice.
- Do not introduce plugin-specific runner fields into DevNexus core; plugins can contribute generic capabilities and profile templates later.

Verification:
- Focused profile schema/status/agent-context tests.
- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus main through the configured automation account after verification passes.

items.title: Record remote execution requests and results
items.description:
Type: remote host execution implementation slice
Readiness: todo/dependent on dev-nexus:local-79 and dev-nexus:local-80
Source PRD: docs/remote-host-execution-prd.md
Parent: dev-nexus:local-77

Goal: add durable request/result records for bounded remote host work while keeping DevNexus coordination state separate from the eventual execution transport.

User stories covered:
- As a Windows agent, I can request Mac-specific verification and later read a structured result without relying on terminal chat.
- As a Mac agent, I can record a Windows-specific verification result with command, commit, exit code, and output tail.

Acceptance criteria:
- DevNexus can create an execution request with project, component, optional work item, requesting host/agent, required capabilities or target host, runner profile, repository/ref, command profile, timeout, expected artifacts, mutation class, and initial status.
- DevNexus can record a result with host id, runner profile id, actual commit/ref, commands run, exit code, verification outcome, output tail, artifact references, cleanup status, and blocker/safety reason when applicable.
- Request statuses include queued, accepted, running, completed, failed, blocked, timed_out, and cancelled.
- CLI and MCP expose request creation and result read/record operations in draft/local form with no remote execution.
- Request/result records can be attached to work items or coordination records without duplicating source-of-truth fields.

Boundaries:
- Do not run local or remote commands in this slice.
- Do not post to live external providers unless an existing provider policy explicitly allows it; local tracker coverage is sufficient.

Verification:
- Focused CLI/MCP/service tests for request creation, status transitions, result recording, malformed output tails, and component/work-item references.
- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus main through the configured automation account after verification passes.

items.title: Add read-only host capability check command
items.description:
Type: remote host execution tracer-bullet implementation slice
Readiness: todo/dependent on dev-nexus:local-79 through dev-nexus:local-81
Source PRD: docs/remote-host-execution-prd.md
Parent: dev-nexus:local-77

Goal: provide the first useful host runner behavior: read-only capability checks for the current host and mocked remote hosts, with structured results recorded through the request/result model.

User stories covered:
- As a coordinator, I can ask for a setup check and see whether generic DevNexus, Node, Git, MCP config, and plugin-declared tools are visible on that host.
- As a maintainer, I can see that a host is unavailable or missing capability facts without treating it as broken.

Acceptance criteria:
- CLI and MCP expose a read-only host check operation that can target local host facts and a mocked remote transport in tests.
- The check reports platform, shell kind, DevNexus CLI availability, Git availability, Node availability when applicable, configured host capabilities, and MCP configuration visibility at a summary level.
- The check records or returns a structured result without exposing absolute personal paths except through host-local logs.
- Missing tools or unavailable hosts produce blocked/unavailable facts, not uncaught transport errors.
- Output is small enough for agent consumption and includes recommended next action.

Boundaries:
- Do not open real SSH/Tailscale connections in default tests.
- Do not install packages, mutate repos, start services, launch images, run Docker, or refresh MCP config in this slice.

Verification:
- Focused tests for local host probe fixtures, mocked remote success/failure, missing command capability, MCP config summary, and redaction of host-local details.
- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus main through the configured automation account after verification passes.

items.title: Plan SSH transport execution without live network dependency
items.description:
Type: remote host execution implementation slice
Readiness: todo/dependent on dev-nexus:local-79 through dev-nexus:local-82
Source PRD: docs/remote-host-execution-prd.md
Parent: dev-nexus:local-77

Goal: add the SSH/Tailscale transport planning layer without requiring a live network connection in normal verification.

User stories covered:
- As an agent, I can see exactly what SSH command would run, which host-local profile it uses, what working directory it expects, and which safety gate blocks it before any remote process starts.
- As a maintainer, I can verify that Tailscale addresses, SSH users, and key aliases stay host-local.

Acceptance criteria:
- DevNexus can build an SSH execution plan from a host id, host-local overlay, runner profile, repository/ref, and command profile.
- The plan includes sanitized command shape, working-directory resolution class, timeout, output-tail policy, and required environment keys without printing secrets or personal absolute paths in shared records.
- Missing SSH overlay, missing Tailscale or host address, missing remote workspace root, and unsupported shell kind produce actionable blocked plans.
- Tests use mocked transport and command runners only.
- The implementation is transport-neutral enough that future local daemon, GitHub runner, or app-server transports can share request/result records.

Boundaries:
- Do not execute real SSH, Tailscale, remote commands, Git fetches, package installs, or live runtime commands in this slice.
- Do not make SSH the only future transport.

Verification:
- Focused SSH plan tests for macOS, Windows, Linux, missing overlay fields, PowerShell/posix shell quoting, timeout policy, and redaction.
- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus main through the configured automation account after verification passes.

items.title: Execute verification-only remote commands through approved runner profiles
items.description:
Type: remote host execution implementation slice
Readiness: todo/dependent on dev-nexus:local-79 through dev-nexus:local-83
Source PRD: docs/remote-host-execution-prd.md
Parent: dev-nexus:local-77

Goal: execute bounded verification-only remote commands through approved runner profiles and record structured results, while preserving publication and automation identity guardrails.

User stories covered:
- As a Windows agent, I can ask a Mac host to verify a specific branch or commit and get pass/fail evidence.
- As a maintainer, I can see which bot or human identity was used for every Git or GitHub operation involved in remote verification.

Acceptance criteria:
- The executor refuses arbitrary freeform commands unless they match an approved command profile or verification profile.
- Remote execution checks out or verifies the requested repository ref before running the command, according to the runner profile's mutation class.
- Agent-created Git and GitHub operations use the configured automation identity; failure to authenticate as the automation actor blocks rather than falling back to the human account.
- Results include actual commit, commands run, exit code, verification status, output tail, and artifact references when configured.
- Timeouts and output limits are enforced and reported as timed_out or failed with actionable summaries.

Boundaries:
- Do not enable live-runtime, package install, Docker, GUI automation, publication, merge, or destructive cleanup operation classes in this slice.
- Live SSH smoke remains a separate policy-gated dogfood item.

Verification:
- Mocked transport tests for pass, fail, timeout, wrong actor, missing command profile, missing ref, and output truncation.
- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus main through the configured automation account after verification passes.

items.title: Choose first Mac Windows remote runner dogfood policy
items.description:
Type: remote host execution rollout decision slice
Readiness: human-in-the-loop (HITL)
Source PRD: docs/remote-host-execution-prd.md open questions
Parent: dev-nexus:local-77

Goal: choose the first real Mac/Windows dogfood policy for remote host execution after the generic request/result and runner surfaces exist.

Decision points:
- Should the first shared host registry live in this dogfood meta project, in DevNexus home-level host config, or both?
- Should the first execution mode be push-based SSH from requester to target, pull-based polling by each host, or both behind the same request/result model?
- Which host ids and capability tags should represent the current Windows and Mac machines?
- Which command profiles are allowed first: setup check only, npm check, Pharo Launcher read-only status, MCP tool listing, or another narrow profile?
- Which auth profiles and bot identities are allowed for any Git/GitHub activity on each host?
- Which quiet-period and unavailable-host behavior should a coordinator follow?

Acceptance criteria:
- Record the chosen host registry location, transport mode, initial host ids, allowed command profiles, auth profile rules, timeout/output limits, and unavailable-host policy.
- Confirm whether live SSH execution is approved for one harmless read-only smoke or whether rollout remains dry-run only.
- Update project config or target state only after the policy is explicit.

Boundaries:
- Do not perform live SSH/Tailscale execution as part of the decision.
- Do not store Tailscale IPs, SSH usernames, keys, personal absolute paths, ports, or secrets in shared project state.

Verification:
- Static policy/config review before live use.
- After approval, one read-only host status smoke through the chosen transport.

Publication notes:
- Dogfood project-state/policy change only unless DevNexus source changes are required later.

items.title: Run first read-only Mac Windows remote host status smoke
items.description:
Type: remote host execution dogfood smoke
Readiness: blocked on dev-nexus:local-79 through dev-nexus:local-83 and explicit live SSH approval from dev-nexus:local-85
Source PRD: docs/remote-host-execution-prd.md rollout plan
Parent: dev-nexus:local-77

Goal: prove the generic remote host execution surface with one harmless read-only status check between the Windows and Mac dogfood machines.

Acceptance criteria:
- The approved runner profile names the exact requester host, target host, transport mode, command profile, timeout, output retention, and cleanup expectation.
- The smoke records a remote execution request, runs the read-only host check, and records a structured result with platform, configured capabilities, DevNexus availability, Git availability, Node availability where relevant, and MCP summary.
- The result is recorded back into DevNexus without relying on this chat transcript.
- Any unavailable host, missing command, or authentication mismatch is recorded as blocked with actionable next steps.
- No packages are installed, no repositories are mutated except any approved read-only fetch/status operation, no services are started, and no live Pharo images, Docker, GUI automation, or cleanup actions run.

Boundaries:
- Requires explicit approval for live SSH/Tailscale use.
- Do not broaden the smoke into verification, package install, runtime, or publication work.

Verification:
- Execute the single approved read-only smoke and inspect the recorded request/result.
- Run no broader checks unless separately approved.

Publication notes:
- Dogfood evidence only unless source changes are needed to fix the runner surface.

items.title: Add actor role and authority configuration model
items.description:
Type: implementation tracer-bullet slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK)
Parent planning item: dev-nexus:local-78
Source PRD: Coordination Roles And Authority PRD

Goal: add the neutral actor, role, action, and scoped authority configuration model without changing publication behavior yet.

User stories covered:
- As a project owner, I can declare that a bot account is a maintainer for my private DevNexus project and a contributor for external repositories.
- As a maintainer, I can define recommended roles without hard-coding dogfood account names.

Acceptance criteria:
- Project configuration accepts logical actors with stable ids, kind, provider identity, display name, and optional handles.
- Project configuration accepts role definitions that expand into explicit authority actions.
- Project configuration accepts scoped role bindings for project, component, provider, tracker, repository, target branch, and environment scopes.
- Built-in recommended role names are available as defaults or examples: maintainer, contributor, reviewer, observer, runtime operator, and release operator.
- Unknown actors normalize to observer authority unless a project explicitly configures another fallback.
- Config validation rejects unknown roles, duplicate actor ids, duplicate role ids, invalid action names, and unusable empty scoped bindings.
- Existing project configs without authority policy continue to load safely.

Boundaries:
- Do not gate commands in this slice.
- Do not perform live provider permission checks.
- Do not store secrets or host-local credential paths in shared project config.

Verification:
- Focused project config and lifecycle tests for default, custom, invalid, and scoped authority policies.
- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus main after verification passes under configured dogfood policy.

items.title: Resolve current actor from host-local auth profiles
items.description:
Type: implementation tracer-bullet slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK) after the authority configuration model exists
Parent planning item: dev-nexus:local-78
Source PRD: Coordination Roles And Authority PRD

Goal: detect or report the current actor from configured host-local auth profiles so agents know whether they are acting as a human, bot, machine user, service account, external agent, or local observer.

User stories covered:
- As a worker agent, I can know whether my Git and provider actions are using the intended bot profile.
- As a human maintainer, I can see account mismatches before automation publishes as the wrong account.

Acceptance criteria:
- Host-local auth profile metadata can bind a logical actor id to non-secret credential mechanisms such as GitHub CLI profile names, SSH host aliases, wrapper command names, or environment keys.
- Actor resolution can identify the configured automation actor for a component or report unknown, ambiguous, missing, or mismatched actor state.
- Human/manual profiles and automation/bot profiles remain distinct and do not silently fall back to each other.
- Status output includes concise warnings for missing auth profile, ambiguous account, mismatched remote, or missing provider capability.
- Resolution is safe when no live provider credentials are available.

Dependencies:
- Depends on the actor/role/authority configuration model.
- Builds on existing publication identity and remote guardrail behavior.

Boundaries:
- Do not require live GitHub, GitLab, or Jira credentials for normal tests.
- Do not mutate credentials, SSH config, or provider profiles.

Verification:
- Focused tests for expected actor, missing actor, ambiguous actor, human-vs-bot mismatch, and unknown local process fallback.
- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus main after verification passes under configured dogfood policy.

items.title: Add effective authority resolver
items.description:
Type: implementation tracer-bullet slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK) after authority config and current-actor resolution exist
Parent planning item: dev-nexus:local-78
Source PRD: Coordination Roles And Authority PRD

Goal: add a pure effective-authority resolver that answers whether a requested action is allowed, blocked, or waiting on provider state.

User stories covered:
- As a coordinator agent, I can know whether I may merge verified DevNexus work directly or must open a pull request.
- As a worker agent, I can choose a safe fallback such as request review or record a handoff when authority is missing.

Acceptance criteria:
- Resolver inputs include actor, auth profile, project, component, provider/tracker/remote/repository scope, target branch, requested action, component publication policy, automation safety profile, and provider state when available.
- Resolver output includes allowed or blocked status, matched actor/role/rule, required missing actions or provider signals, recommended fallback action, and concise explanation.
- Scope precedence is deterministic across project default, component override, provider override, tracker override, branch override, and environment/runtime override.
- Direct integration requires both component publication policy and actor authority.
- Pull request review approval and issue-level design approval remain distinct concepts.
- Assignment, casual comments, and silence are not treated as approval by default.

Dependencies:
- Depends on authority configuration and actor resolution.

Boundaries:
- Keep resolver pure and unit-testable.
- Do not perform command gating or live provider calls in this slice.

Verification:
- Focused resolver tests for maintainer direct integration, contributor pull-request-only, reviewer approval-only, observer read-only, runtime operator without merge authority, and release operator without implementation authority.
- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus main after verification passes under configured dogfood policy.

items.title: Surface effective authority in status reports and agent context
items.description:
Type: implementation tracer-bullet slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK) after the effective authority resolver exists
Parent planning item: dev-nexus:local-78
Source PRD: Coordination Roles And Authority PRD

Goal: make authority visible before agents take action by projecting effective authority into project status, target reports, coordination status, and generated worker context.

User stories covered:
- As a worker agent, I can see whether I am expected to integrate, open a pull request, request review, or only hand off.
- As a human maintainer, I can inspect a target report and see why an agent did or did not publish.

Acceptance criteria:
- Project status summarizes current actor identity when detectable, auth profile name, component role bindings, and key allowed actions.
- Automation target reports and target-cycle facts can include concise authority decisions and blockers.
- Generated worker context and briefings include component-specific publication/coordination authority and safe fallback instructions.
- Coordination status reports authority next to branch, tracker, handoff, and integration facts.
- Low-token summaries remain concise and do not include secrets, full tokens, private key paths, or host-local credential material.

Dependencies:
- Depends on the effective authority resolver.

Boundaries:
- Reporting only; do not block commands in this slice.
- Do not require live provider credentials.

Verification:
- Focused tests for project status, target report, coordination status, and worker context authority summaries.
- Snapshot or text-output tests should check for concise allowed/blocked/fallback information without secrets.
- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus main after verification passes under configured dogfood policy.

items.title: Gate publication actions with authority checks
items.description:
Type: implementation tracer-bullet slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK) after effective authority resolution exists
Parent planning item: dev-nexus:local-78
Source PRD: Coordination Roles And Authority PRD

Goal: make publication helpers and automation publication decisions refuse unsafe push, pull request, merge, approval, and publish actions when the current actor lacks authority.

User stories covered:
- As a coordinator agent, I can directly integrate verified work only when the project says my actor is allowed to do so.
- As an open-source contributor agent, I can prepare a branch and pull request without needing maintainer permissions.

Acceptance criteria:
- Direct target-branch push is blocked unless component publication policy, actor authority, and resolved auth profile all allow it.
- Pull request or merge request creation is allowed for contributor-style actors when configured, and is recommended as fallback when direct integration is blocked.
- Pull request or merge request merge is blocked unless merge authority, provider approval state, required checks, branch policy, and component publication policy all allow it.
- Package or release publication is gated separately from merge authority.
- Blocked publication produces a non-interactive, machine-readable result and a human-readable fallback suggestion.
- Existing direct-integration dogfood behavior remains possible for configured maintainer bots.

Dependencies:
- Depends on effective authority resolution and current actor detection.

Boundaries:
- Use mocked provider/merge state in tests.
- Do not self-approve pull requests unless a later explicit policy item allows it.
- Do not require live GitHub credentials for the first implementation.

Verification:
- Focused publication tests for direct push allowed, direct push blocked with PR fallback, PR-only contributor, merge blocked by missing approval, merge blocked by failed checks, release publish blocked without release authority, and dogfood maintainer merge allowed.
- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus main after verification passes under configured dogfood policy.

items.title: Gate coordination and provider mutation actions with authority checks
items.description:
Type: implementation tracer-bullet slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK) after effective authority resolution exists
Parent planning item: dev-nexus:local-78
Source PRD: Coordination Roles And Authority PRD

Goal: apply authority checks to coordination handoff, integration planning, coordination requests, provider comments, labels, assignments, transitions, approvals, and review decisions.

User stories covered:
- As a coordinator agent, I can draft or post a provider request only when policy allows the underlying action.
- As a reviewer, I can approve or request changes without receiving merge authority.

Acceptance criteria:
- Read-only coordination status and mutation-free integration planning remain available to observer-style actors.
- Coordination handoff, work-item comments, provider comments, labels, assignments, workflow transitions, and review approvals each check the relevant authority action before mutation.
- Coordination request can draft when provider posting is disallowed, and can post only when provider mutation authority allows it.
- Review approval, requested changes, and merge authority remain separate decisions.
- Blocked coordination mutation records a clear reason and suggested fallback without silently dropping the coordination fact.

Dependencies:
- Depends on effective authority resolution.
