- Builds on coordination tracker role selection and external request surfaces.

Boundaries:
- No live provider posting is required for this slice.
- Do not infer approval from assignment, silence, or casual comments.

Verification:
- Focused coordination tests for observer read-only status, contributor handoff allowed but provider label blocked, draft-only request, provider-comment allowed, assignment blocked, reviewer approval allowed, and merge still blocked after reviewer approval.
- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus main after verification passes under configured dogfood policy.

items.title: Map provider approval and branch-policy signals into authority state
items.description:
Type: implementation tracer-bullet slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK) after the authority resolver and provider-neutral signal shape exist
Parent planning item: dev-nexus:local-78
Source PRD: Coordination Roles And Authority PRD

Goal: add mocked provider adapters or adapter helpers that translate provider-native review, check, branch-policy, issue-status, and workflow signals into neutral DevNexus authority signals.

User stories covered:
- As an integration agent, I can understand whether provider approval, checks, branch policy, and DevNexus publication policy all allow merging.
- As a worker agent, I can see that an issue-level design approval and a pull-request merge approval are different signals.

Acceptance criteria:
- Neutral provider authority signals include waiting_for_approval, approved, changes_requested, rejected, checks_pending, checks_failed, checks_passed, branch_policy_blocked, mergeable, merge_conflict, and timed_out.
- GitHub-style mocked provider state maps pull request reviews, required checks, mergeability, branch protection, labels, and project-status signals into neutral signals.
- GitLab-style mocked provider state maps merge request approvals, pipeline state, mergeability, labels, and status transitions into neutral signals.
- Jira-style mocked provider state maps workflow transitions, assignees, comments, and status categories into neutral signals for issue-level decisions.
- Assignment remains ownership or expected-responder metadata unless a project explicitly overrides it.
- Provider signal summaries can be consumed by the effective authority resolver without provider-specific branching in command code.

Dependencies:
- Depends on effective authority resolver concepts and provider abstraction points.

Boundaries:
- Mocked providers only for the first implementation.
- Do not add webhooks or continuous polling.

Verification:
- Focused provider-signal mapping tests for GitHub, GitLab, Jira, ambiguous signals, assignment-not-approval, and branch-policy blockers.
- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus main after verification passes under configured dogfood policy.

items.title: Document authority roles for private bots and open-source contributors
items.description:
Type: documentation and onboarding slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK) after the implemented authority configuration shape stabilizes
Parent planning item: dev-nexus:local-78
Source PRD: Coordination Roles And Authority PRD

Goal: document how users configure agent roles and authority for private DevNexus projects, bot accounts, existing project onboarding, and open-source contribution workflows.

User stories covered:
- As a project owner, I can configure a machine-user bot as maintainer for my private meta-project.
- As an open-source contributor, I can use DevNexus to prepare work and propose pull requests without adding DevNexus artifacts or needing maintainer rights.

Acceptance criteria:
- Documentation explains actors, auth profiles, roles, action grants, scoped role bindings, effective authority, and safe defaults.
- Documentation includes generic examples for maintainer bot, contributor-only bot, reviewer, observer, runtime operator, and release operator roles.
- Documentation explains human/manual profile versus automation/bot profile separation and account mismatch warnings.
- Onboarding guidance covers project creation and joining an existing project on a new machine without storing secrets in the shared meta-project.
- Open-source contribution guidance explains PR-only behavior, review waits, no direct integration by default, and no DevNexus artifact contamination of target repositories.
- Dogfood-specific account names appear only in dogfood-specific examples or state, not in generic user-facing docs.

Dependencies:
- Depends on the stabilized authority configuration and status/reporting shape.

Boundaries:
- Do not document unsupported live provider behavior as available.
- Do not include secrets, personal tokens, or private SSH key paths.

Verification:
- Static documentation review.
- Run repository documentation/check command when available.
- npm run check when feasible if docs are included in checks.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus main after verification passes under configured dogfood policy.

items.title: Decide advanced authority policies for self-approval and temporary elevation
items.description:
Type: product policy decision slice
Readiness: human-in-the-loop (HITL)
Parent planning item: dev-nexus:local-78
Source PRD: Coordination Roles And Authority PRD

Goal: decide advanced authority policy questions that should not be guessed by implementation agents.

Questions to resolve:
- Should DevNexus ship built-in role definitions, or only recommended role names that projects define explicitly?
- Should direct integration require separate maintainer and publish roles, or is one maintainer role enough for normal projects?
- Should a maintainer bot ever be allowed to self-approve a pull request, or should approval and merge authority require different actors by default?
- How should temporary authority elevation for a single target cycle be represented and audited?
- Should project-local role policy be mirrored to shared trackers for external participants, or kept only in the DevNexus meta-project?

Acceptance criteria:
- Record decisions in the PRD, project docs, or configuration guidance.
- Identify any follow-up implementation issues created by the decisions.
- Keep conservative defaults until decisions are explicit.

Boundaries:
- Do not implement self-approval, broad live permission probing, or temporary elevation before this policy is resolved.
- Do not require provider credentials to answer the policy questions.

Verification:
- Static policy review against the PRD and current dogfood target state.

Publication notes:
- Dogfood planning state can remain in the meta-project; source changes require a follow-up implementation item.

items.title: Slice parallel agent Git workflow PRD into authority-aware work items
items.description:
Type: planning slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK)
Source PRD: docs/parallel-agent-git-workflow-prd.md
Related PRD: docs/coordination-roles-authority-prd.md

Goal: slice the Parallel Agent Git Workflow PRD into bounded DevNexus work items that complement, rather than duplicate, the coordination roles and authority work.

Acceptance criteria:
- Compare the parallel-agent PRD against the authority PRD and existing dev-nexus:local-87 through dev-nexus:local-95.
- Keep actor identity, roles, provider mutation, publication gating, approval signals, and direct integration authority assigned to the authority work items.
- Create parallel-agent issues only for work ownership, shared-checkout boundaries, worktree leases, start/adopt flow, coordination visibility, and no-loss cleanup.
- Record dependencies from parallel-agent issues to authority items where mutation authority is required.

Verification:
- Created work items can be listed through the DevNexus work-item CLI.
- No live providers, SSH, Tailscale, Pharo, PLexus, Docker, package installs, or destructive cleanup are used.

Publication notes:
- Dogfood planning state stays in the meta project until sliced source work lands through the DevNexus component publication policy.

items.title: Document authority-aware worktree-first chat workflow
items.description:
Type: documentation and agent-workflow slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK)
Source PRD: docs/parallel-agent-git-workflow-prd.md
Related authority work: dev-nexus:local-87 through dev-nexus:local-95
Parent planning item: dev-nexus:local-96

Goal: document the worktree-first default for interactive DevNexus/Codex chats while explaining how authority roles remain a separate policy layer.

User stories covered:
- As a human, I can start several chats in one project without remembering which branch each chat should use.
- As a worker agent, I know that shared checkouts are read-mostly and generated worktrees are the normal writable implementation surface.

Acceptance criteria:
- User and agent-facing docs state that mutating interactive chats should prepare or adopt an isolated component or project-meta worktree before editing.
- Docs distinguish shared checkouts, worker worktrees, integration branches, rescue branches, and stable component source roots.
- Docs explain worker versus integrator behavior without creating a second authority role model.
- Docs explicitly defer actor identity, direct integration, pull requests, provider mutation, approval, and publication permission to the authority model from dev-nexus:local-87 through dev-nexus:local-95.
- Docs include no-loss expectations: commit or hand off useful work before ending, do not delete ambiguous branches/worktrees, and use cleanup dry-run first.
- Generic docs avoid dogfood-specific account names except in dogfood-only examples.

Boundaries:
- Do not document enforcement as implemented before the corresponding source slices land.
- Do not change runtime, provider, or publication behavior in this slice.

Verification:
- Static documentation review.
- npm run check when feasible if repository checks cover docs.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus main after verification passes under configured dogfood policy.

items.title: Add fail-closed shared-checkout mutation guard
items.description:
Type: implementation fail-safe slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK)
Source PRD: docs/parallel-agent-git-workflow-prd.md
Parent planning item: dev-nexus:local-96

Goal: make DevNexus-controlled mutating flows fail closed before they edit a shared project or component checkout. This is a tool-level guard, not an agent-instruction preference.

User stories covered:
- As a worker agent, I am blocked before editing the shared checkout and receive the safe next action to prepare or adopt an owned worktree.
- As a human maintainer, I can start multiple chats without relying on prompt compliance to protect the meta-project main branch.
- As an integrator, I can still perform an explicit integration action after the tool verifies that the role/action is allowed.

Acceptance criteria:
- DevNexus can classify the target Git checkout as shared project checkout, shared component checkout, generated component worktree, generated project-meta worktree, integration worktree, bootstrap/setup operation, or unknown.
- DevNexus-controlled write operations call the guard before mutation, including local work-item writes, target-state/project-state writes, skill/MCP projection refreshes, coordination handoffs that write project state, publication/integration operations, and cleanup execution.
- Shared-checkout writes are refused by default with a non-interactive machine-readable result that names the target, mutation class, classification, and safer next action.
- The guard allows read-only commands and the narrow bootstrap operations required to create/adopt an owned worktree or inspect coordination state.
- The first implementation uses explicit command/action allowlists and explicit overrides for integrator/bootstrap cases; later authority work can replace or refine the override mechanism.
- Branch name alone is never treated as proof of isolation; a writable worker surface requires an owned worktree or explicit integration/bootstrap classification.
- Tests cover local tracker mutation from shared meta checkout, target-state mutation from shared meta checkout, component source mutation from shared component checkout, generated component worktree success, generated project-meta worktree success, bootstrap worktree prepare success, unknown checkout refusal, explicit override, and integration-role allowance.

Dependencies:
- Should land before dev-nexus:local-99 through local-101 become the common workflow path.
- Does not block on authority model completion; authority can be layered onto this fail-safe after dev-nexus:local-87 through local-89.
- Complements dev-nexus:local-91 and dev-nexus:local-92; do not duplicate publication or provider mutation permission policy there.

Boundaries:
- Do not introduce hard locks between independent owned worktrees.
- Do not block read-only status, setup check, coordination status, or integration planning.
- Do not require live provider credentials.
- Do not infer implementation ownership from branch name alone.

Verification:
- Focused checkout-classification and command-boundary tests.
- Focused CLI/MCP tests proving mutating tools fail before writing shared checkout state.
- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus main after verification passes under configured dogfood policy.

items.title: Record advisory worktree leases for active agent work
items.description:
Type: implementation tracer-bullet slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK)
Source PRD: docs/parallel-agent-git-workflow-prd.md
Parent planning item: dev-nexus:local-96

Goal: add advisory worktree lease records so parallel chats and multi-host agents can see active ownership intent without introducing mandatory hard locks.

User stories covered:
- As a Mac agent, I can see that a Windows agent is already working on a related component surface before I start editing.
- As a maintainer, I can inspect one coordination surface and understand what is working, ready, blocked, merged, stale, or abandoned.

Acceptance criteria:
- DevNexus can create or refresh a lease for component or project-meta work with project id, host id, optional agent/chat id, component or meta scope, optional work item, branch, base ref, intended write scope, status, timestamps, and notes.
- Lease status supports working, ready, blocked, integrating, merged, abandoned, and stale.
- Lease records include last observed head commit, dirty state, and pushed state when Git facts are available.
- Leases are advisory freshness signals; stale or conflicting leases warn but do not become hard locks by default.
- Worktree prepare and coordination handoff can attach or update lease information when policy allows.
- Tests cover lease creation, refresh, stale detection, status transitions, component and project-meta scopes, and missing host or agent ids.

Dependencies:
- Builds on existing worktree prepare and coordination handoff behavior from dev-nexus:local-14, dev-nexus:local-15, and dev-nexus:local-19.
- Can later include current actor details from dev-nexus:local-88 without blocking the first lease model.

Boundaries:
- Do not block work solely because a lease exists.
- Do not store absolute personal paths in shared lease records; use project-relative or classified paths where possible.
- Do not require a shared external provider in the first implementation.

Verification:
- Focused lease model, lifecycle, and coordination integration tests.
- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus main after verification passes under configured dogfood policy.

items.title: Expand coordination status for active parallel work
items.description:
Type: implementation slice
Readiness: todo/dependent on advisory worktree leases and authority status summaries
Source PRD: docs/parallel-agent-git-workflow-prd.md
Parent planning item: dev-nexus:local-96

Goal: make coordination status answer what active work may affect the current agent before it edits, hands off, integrates, or cleans up.

User stories covered:
- As a worker agent, I can see related active branches and likely overlap before starting work.
- As an integration agent, I can see ready branches, verification state, likely conflicts, and suggested next action.

Acceptance criteria:
- coordination status reports active and stale leases, ready handoffs, dirty shared checkouts, dirty generated worktrees, unpushed commits, branches ahead/behind target, and missing upstream branches.
- Status groups related branches by component, work item, branch naming hints, handoff records, lease records, and changed-area overlap.
- Status reports likely overlap at file/package/component granularity without claiming semantic conflict resolution.
- When authority summaries are available, status shows whether the current actor can read, hand off, push branch, integrate, clean up, or must fall back to a request or handoff.
- Output includes a concise next action such as prepare worktree, continue owned lease, wait, integrate, push branch, record handoff, or run cleanup dry-run.
- Tests cover no active work, one owned lease, another-host active lease, stale lease, dirty shared checkout, unpushed branch, overlapping changed areas, and missing authority summary.

Dependencies:
- Depends on dev-nexus:local-99 for lease records.
- Depends on dev-nexus:local-90 for authority summaries when available, but should degrade gracefully before authority reporting is complete.

Boundaries:
- Read-only by default.
- Do not create or delete branches, worktrees, provider records, or leases from status alone.

Verification:
- Focused coordination status tests with synthetic Git and tracker fixtures.
- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus main after verification passes under configured dogfood policy.

items.title: Add start or adopt flow for interactive chats
items.description:
Type: implementation slice
Readiness: todo/dependent on leases, coordination status, and authority resolution
Source PRD: docs/parallel-agent-git-workflow-prd.md
Parent planning item: dev-nexus:local-96

Goal: give an interactive chat one low-friction DevNexus flow that either prepares a safe worktree or adopts an existing owned worktree before mutation starts.

User stories covered:
- As a human, I can start several chats in one project without remembering which branch each chat should use.
- As a worker agent, I can ask DevNexus for a safe starting point and receive a prepared worktree, branch, ownership metadata, and write-scope guidance.

Acceptance criteria:
- The flow inspects current project, component, work item or topic, Git checkout classification, active leases, handoffs, branch state, and effective authority when available.
- For new work, it prepares a component or project-meta worktree with branch name, context bundle, write-scope metadata, and initial lease.
- For existing owned work, it can adopt an active lease/worktree after verifying the branch, worktree path, dirty state, and requested scope still match.
- For conflicting or unsafe work, it reports a clear blocked result with alternatives such as choose another work item, continue existing branch, wait for handoff, or run integration planning.
- The flow can run in dry-run mode to explain what it would do without creating a branch or lease.
- Tests cover fresh work item, topic-only project-meta work, adopting owned work, conflicting lease, shared checkout mutation attempt, missing component, and blocked authority.

Dependencies:
- Depends on dev-nexus:local-98 for shared-checkout boundary checks.
- Depends on dev-nexus:local-99 and dev-nexus:local-100 for leases and status.
- Depends on dev-nexus:local-89 for effective authority before mutating shared or provider-backed state.

Boundaries:
- Do not make DevNexus choose which implementation work should be done; the caller still supplies or selects intent.
- Do not launch live runtime, package install, provider mutation, or remote host execution.

Verification:
- Focused start/adopt CLI and service tests.
- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus main after verification passes under configured dogfood policy.

items.title: Add branch and worktree cleanup dry-run safety classifier
items.description:
Type: implementation tracer-bullet slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK)
Source PRD: docs/parallel-agent-git-workflow-prd.md
Parent planning item: dev-nexus:local-96

Goal: provide a read-only cleanup plan that classifies generated worktrees and branches by safety before any deletion or pruning is allowed.

User stories covered:
- As a cleanup agent, I can prune old worktrees and branches without deleting unmerged or unpushed work.
- As an agent, I can preserve useful uncommitted work by moving it to a rescue branch instead of leaving it hidden in a dirty checkout.

Acceptance criteria:
- Cleanup dry-run scans configured component and project-meta worktree roots plus known codex/dev-nexus branches for the selected scope.
- Each branch/worktree is classified as safe, blocked, stale, merged, superseded, dirty, unpushed, missing handoff, unknown merge state, active lease, abandoned, or needs rescue.
- The classifier checks dirty files, untracked files, unpushed commits, upstream existence, merge-base containment, handoff status, lease status, and known publication target where available.
- The plan explains the proof that makes a candidate safe or the missing evidence that blocks cleanup.
- The plan is read-only and can output structured JSON for automation.
- Tests cover clean merged branch, dirty worktree, untracked files, unpushed commits, missing upstream, stale lease, ready handoff, superseded commit, and unknown target branch.

Dependencies:
- Builds on existing Git worktree and coordination handoff services.
- Can consume lease records from dev-nexus:local-99 when available, but should report no-lease state safely before then.

Boundaries:
- Do not delete branches or worktrees in this slice.
- Do not assume branch naming alone proves safety.
- Do not require live provider credentials.

Verification:
- Focused cleanup classification tests with synthetic Git fixtures.
- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus main after verification passes under configured dogfood policy.

items.title: Execute cleanup with rescue preservation under authority gates
items.description:
Type: implementation slice
Readiness: todo/dependent on cleanup dry-run and effective authority resolution
Source PRD: docs/parallel-agent-git-workflow-prd.md
Parent planning item: dev-nexus:local-96

Goal: execute only proven-safe branch and worktree cleanup while preserving ambiguous or valuable work through rescue branches or archive records.

User stories covered:
- As a cleanup agent, I can remove stale generated worktrees without losing work.
- As a maintainer, I can see why a branch was deleted, preserved, rescued, or blocked.

Acceptance criteria:
- Cleanup execution requires a prior cleanup plan or recomputes the plan immediately before mutation.
- Execution removes only candidates classified as safe under the selected policy and current effective authority.
- Dirty, unpushed, unknown, active, or missing-handoff candidates are refused by default.
- When policy allows rescue, DevNexus can create a rescue branch or archive record before removing an abandoned worktree.
- Cleanup records results with branch/worktree identity, proof, actor authority summary, actions taken, skipped candidates, and recovery guidance.
- Tests cover safe removal, blocked removal, rescue branch creation, authority-blocked cleanup, stale plan refusal, and idempotent reruns.

Dependencies:
- Depends on dev-nexus:local-102 for cleanup dry-run classification.
- Depends on dev-nexus:local-89 for effective authority resolution.
- Depends on dev-nexus:local-92 or equivalent authority actions for coordination/provider cleanup records when provider mutation is involved.

Boundaries:
- Do not delete source checkouts or non-generated worktrees unless explicitly classified and authorized.
- Do not perform destructive cleanup without a machine-readable proof record.
- Do not require live provider credentials for local cleanup tests.

Verification:
- Focused cleanup execution and rescue tests.
- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus main after verification passes under configured dogfood policy.

items.title: Make agent-native projections opt-in by active target
items.description:
Type: projection policy / setup UX PRD follow-up
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK) for issue slicing and first implementation planning
Source PRD: docs/agent-target-projection-opt-in-prd.md

Goal: make agent-native MCP, skills, plugin, and worker support projections opt-in by active agent target so Codex-only projects do not receive Claude artifacts and vice versa.

Current dogfood finding:
- The dogfood project MCP target is Codex-only.
- The dogfood project skills targets currently include Codex and Claude, so DevNexus generated ignored `.claude/skills` support state.
- This is not tracked source, but it is root clutter and a misleading active-provider signal for a Codex-only project.

Acceptance criteria:
- Use the PRD to slice implementation-ready DevNexus work items.
- The design distinguishes supported providers from active agent targets.
- MCP, skills, plugin capability projection, setup checks, and worker context generation consume one active target policy.
- Setup reports stale generated provider projections and can safely clean generated support state when no longer selected.
- Codex-only projects do not receive `.claude` support directories by default.
- Multi-provider projects can still explicitly opt into Codex plus Claude or other provider combinations.

Boundaries:
- Do not remove provider support.
- Do not delete manually authored or source-controlled provider files automatically.
- Do not edit provider-global credentials or user-global skill directories.
- Keep this generic; do not hard-code Codex-only behavior into DevNexus core.

Verification:
- After slicing, implementation issues should cover config normalization, projection filtering, setup diagnostics, cleanup dry-run/execution, worker context behavior, and docs.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus main for source changes after focused and full checks pass under dogfood policy.

items.title: Add active agent target policy and compatibility normalization
items.description:
Type: implementation tracer-bullet slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK)
Parent planning item: dev-nexus:local-104
Source PRD: docs/agent-target-projection-opt-in-prd.md

Goal: add one explicit active-agent-target policy that distinguishes supported providers from providers selected for this project or host.

User stories covered:
- As a Codex-only project owner, I can configure DevNexus so only Codex-native project files are generated.
- As a multi-provider team, we can explicitly select Codex and Claude and get both projections intentionally.

Acceptance criteria:
- Project configuration accepts an active agent target policy with provider id, enabled state, optional MCP settings, optional skill projection settings, source-control policy, and setup notes.
- Existing projects using only mcp.agentTargets and skills.agentTargets continue to load through compatibility normalization.
- Normalization preserves intentionally configured multi-provider projects, while allowing setup/status to recommend an explicit active target set.
- New project/setup defaults can represent the provider chosen during setup without enabling every supported provider.
- Invalid duplicate, unknown, disabled, or unsupported active target configurations fail with actionable errors.

Boundaries:
- Do not remove Codex, Claude, OpenCode, or manual/custom provider support.
- Do not change projection materialization behavior in this slice beyond normalized configuration surfaces.
- Do not write provider-global state or credentials.

Verification:
- Focused project config/lifecycle tests for Codex-only, Claude-only, multi-provider, legacy MCP-only, legacy skills-only, and invalid target configs.
- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus main after verification passes under dogfood policy.

items.title: Filter MCP skills and plugin projections by active agent targets
items.description:
Type: implementation tracer-bullet slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK) after active agent target policy exists
Parent planning item: dev-nexus:local-104
Source PRD: docs/agent-target-projection-opt-in-prd.md

Goal: make MCP config refresh, skill projection, plugin projected skills, plugin MCP entries, and setup fragments materialize only for active compatible agent targets.

User stories covered:
- As a Codex-only project owner, I do not receive Claude-native support directories.
- As a plugin author, I can declare target-compatible capabilities without forcing every compatible provider to be materialized.

Acceptance criteria:
- MCP refresh defaults to the active target set unless the command explicitly names a provider target.
- Skill refresh defaults to the active target set and does not create target directories outside that set.
- Plugin capabilities declare compatible target agents, not mandatory target agents, and are materialized only when compatible with an active target.
- Explicit command-line target selection can refresh a selected provider without mutating unrelated provider directories.
- Codex-only fixture tests prove no `.claude` support directory is created; Claude-only fixture tests prove no Codex-native support is created unless selected; multi-provider tests still create both.

Dependencies:
- Depends on dev-nexus:local-105 active target policy.

Boundaries:
- Do not delete existing stale directories in this slice; only stop creating newly unselected projections.
- Do not weaken provider trust or approval settings.

Verification:
- Focused MCP config, skill materialization, plugin capability, and setup assistant tests.
- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus main after verification passes under dogfood policy.

items.title: Report stale and unexpected provider projections in setup and status
items.description:
Type: implementation tracer-bullet slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK) after active target policy and projection filtering exist
Parent planning item: dev-nexus:local-104
Source PRD: docs/agent-target-projection-opt-in-prd.md

Goal: make setup checks and project status distinguish expected, missing, stale generated, manual, and unsupported provider-native projections.

User stories covered:
- As a coordinator agent, I can see whether a provider-native directory is expected, stale generated support, or manually authored.
- As a new-machine setup user, I can avoid misreading stale provider support as an active workflow.

Acceptance criteria:
- Project status summarizes active agent targets, expected MCP config files, expected skill directories, selected plugin capabilities, unsupported/manual targets, and stale generated provider directories.
- Setup check reports expected-present, expected-missing, present-stale-generated, present-manual, unsupported-provider, and locally-selected-but-not-allowed states.
- Stale generated support is detected using generated markers, projection manifests, known ignored paths, or another safe evidence source.
- Manual or source-controlled provider files are reported without being classified as cleanup-safe.
- Output remains concise enough for agent consumption and does not include secrets or provider-global credentials.

Dependencies:
- Depends on dev-nexus:local-105 and dev-nexus:local-106.

Boundaries:
- Reporting only; do not remove files in this slice.
- Do not inspect provider-global app databases or credentials.

Verification:
- Focused status/setup tests for expected, missing, stale generated, manual, unsupported, and host-local override cases.
- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus main after verification passes under dogfood policy.

items.title: Add safe cleanup for stale generated agent provider support
items.description:
Type: implementation tracer-bullet slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK) after stale projection diagnostics exist
Parent planning item: dev-nexus:local-104
Source PRD: docs/agent-target-projection-opt-in-prd.md

Goal: provide a dry-run-first cleanup path for generated provider support directories that are no longer selected by active agent targets.

User stories covered:
- As a Codex-only project owner, I can remove stale generated Claude support without risking manually authored files.
- As a multi-provider team, intentionally active provider support is preserved.

Acceptance criteria:
- Cleanup dry-run lists stale generated provider support paths, why they are considered generated, source-control state, and whether cleanup is safe.
- Cleanup execution removes only cleanup-safe generated support paths when explicitly requested.
- Cleanup refuses source-controlled, unknown, manually authored, or provider-global files unless a future explicit force policy exists.
- Cleanup updates or recommends refreshing setup/status state after removal.
- Errors are non-interactive and actionable for automation.

Dependencies:
- Depends on dev-nexus:local-107 stale projection diagnostics.

Boundaries:
- Do not delete user-global skills, provider credentials, app databases, or unknown provider directories.
- Do not remove active selected provider projections.

Verification:
- Focused cleanup tests for dry-run, safe removal, source-controlled refusal, unknown/manual refusal, active-provider preservation, and idempotent rerun.
- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus main after verification passes under dogfood policy.

items.title: Propagate active agent targets into worker context preparation
items.description:
Type: implementation tracer-bullet slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK) after active target policy and projection filtering exist
Parent planning item: dev-nexus:local-104
Source PRD: docs/agent-target-projection-opt-in-prd.md

Goal: ensure generated worker context and prepared worktree support files describe and materialize only the provider-native projections relevant to the worker agent target.

User stories covered:
- As a worker agent, I receive the skill and MCP context for the provider I am actually using.
- As a coordinator agent, I can prepare a Codex worker without also creating Claude-native support state.

Acceptance criteria:
- Worker context records the project active target policy and the assigned worker provider target.
- Worker-local skill projections and plugin fragments are filtered by the assigned provider target.
- Worker briefing describes only relevant provider-native directories plus shared project-managed skill roots.
- Preparing a worktree for a provider not active in project policy fails or produces a clear manual-target warning according to configuration.
- Multi-provider projects can prepare distinct worker contexts for different active providers without cross-contaminating support files.

Dependencies:
- Depends on dev-nexus:local-105 and dev-nexus:local-106.

Boundaries:
- Do not create nested execution workspaces or change the Git worktree model.
- Do not install global skills or write to user-global provider directories.

Verification:
- Focused worker context and worktree prepare tests for Codex-only, Claude-only, multi-provider, unsupported provider, and plugin fragment filtering cases.
- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus main after verification passes under dogfood policy.

items.title: Document opt-in agent targets and projection cleanup
items.description:
Type: documentation and onboarding slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK) after active target behavior stabilizes
Parent planning item: dev-nexus:local-104
Source PRD: docs/agent-target-projection-opt-in-prd.md

Goal: document supported providers versus active targets, provider-specific projection behavior, and stale generated support cleanup for new and existing DevNexus projects.

User stories covered:
- As a Codex-only project owner, I can understand why only Codex-native files are generated.
- As a Claude or OpenCode user, I can opt into the provider I actually use.
- As a multi-provider team, we can intentionally configure multiple active targets.

Acceptance criteria:
- User docs explain supported provider versus active target, MCP projection, skill projection, plugin compatibility, and worker context projection.
- Docs include Codex-only, Claude-only, OpenCode/manual, and multi-provider examples.
- Setup/onboarding docs explain how to choose active targets when creating or joining a project.
- Cleanup docs explain stale generated support detection, dry-run, cleanup execution, and refusal of manual/source-controlled files.
- Generic docs avoid dogfood-specific account names or implying Codex is universal default.

Dependencies:
- Depends on the implemented active target and cleanup behavior.

Boundaries:
- Do not document unsupported live provider capability discovery.
- Do not include personal paths, credentials, or provider-global details.

Verification:
- Static documentation review.
- npm run check when feasible if docs participate in checks.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus main after verification passes under dogfood policy.

items.title: Migrate dogfood project to explicit Codex-only active targets
items.description:
Type: dogfood migration slice
Readiness: todo/dependent after active target filtering, stale diagnostics, and cleanup behavior are available
Parent planning item: dev-nexus:local-104
Source PRD: docs/agent-target-projection-opt-in-prd.md

Goal: migrate this dogfood project to an explicit Codex-only active target policy and remove stale ignored Claude support generated from the old skills target list.

Acceptance criteria:
- Update dogfood project configuration so the active agent target policy reflects the current Codex-only workflow.
- Keep DevNexus support for Claude and other providers available but inactive for this project until explicitly selected.
- Refresh MCP and skill projections after the config change.
- Setup/status reports Codex projections as expected and `.claude/skills` as absent or cleaned, not active.
- Remove `.claude/skills` only through the safe generated-support cleanup path or after verifying it is ignored generated support and not source-controlled/manual.
- Run setup check and project status after migration.

Dependencies:
- Depends on dev-nexus:local-105 through at least dev-nexus:local-108.

Boundaries:
- Do not remove Claude support from DevNexus source.
- Do not delete manually authored provider files.
- Do not touch user-global Claude or Codex directories.

Verification:
- dev-nexus setup check . join-existing-project --platform windows --json
- dev-nexus project status . --json
- git status summary showing only intended dogfood meta changes.

Publication notes:
