items.title: Reject plugin MCP tool overlap with DevNexus core tools
items.description:
Type: core guardrail slice
Readiness: todo/dependent after DevNexus-Pharo default MCP overlap is removed
Depends on: dev-nexus-pharo:local-18
Source: user direction on 2026-05-18: plugins are additive and must not re-expose DevNexus core tools.

Goal:
Add a generic DevNexus guardrail that detects plugin-projected MCP tool names overlapping DevNexus core MCP tools so future plugins cannot recreate a replacement/specialization surface accidentally.

Acceptance criteria:
- DevNexus has a generic additive-plugin validation or setup/status diagnostic for plugin MCP server capabilities whose declared tool names duplicate DevNexus core MCP tool names.
- The diagnostic names the plugin id, server name, duplicate tool names, and replacement owner: use dev_nexus for generic DevNexus operations.
- The guardrail is blocking for generated setup/status or project validation once current plugin surfaces are cleaned; there is no compatibility allow-list for old DevNexus-Pharo names.
- Tests cover a clean additive plugin, a plugin duplicating work_item_list, a plugin duplicating project_status, and multiple plugin MCP servers.
- Documentation states that plugin MCP servers are additive domain surfaces, not alternate generic DevNexus servers.

Boundaries:
- Keep DevNexus core generic; do not hardcode Pharo-specific plugin behavior.
- Do not inspect live external MCP servers or run plugin commands; use declared capability metadata and the DevNexus core tool list.

Verification:
- Run focused DevNexus plugin capability/config/setup tests.
- Run npm run check in DevNexus when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus main through the configured automation account after verification passes.

items.title: Fix Codex app-server stdio wire protocol compatibility
items.description:
Type: implementation defect slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK)
Source: docs/codex-architecture-design-audit-prd.md; follow-up to completed Codex app-server adapter work local-31 through local-35.

Problem:
The current DevNexus Codex app-server transport was added under an incompatible protocol assumption. Current Codex app-server stdio speaks newline-delimited JSON-RPC-lite messages and omits the `jsonrpc` field, while DevNexus currently writes LSP-style `Content-Length` frames and requires `jsonrpc: "2.0"` in responses.

Goal:
Make the existing Codex app-server client and stdio transport compatible with the installed/current Codex app-server wire protocol before any higher-level worker orchestration is treated as operational.

User stories covered:
- As a DevNexus user, I can run an app-server readiness path against the installed Codex binary without failing on framing or JSON-RPC envelope shape.
- As a coordinator, I get a clear protocol compatibility error when a configured Codex binary speaks an unsupported protocol.

Acceptance criteria:
- Codex app-server stdio transport writes one JSON object per line and reads one JSON object per line.
- Requests, responses, notifications, and errors are accepted without requiring a `jsonrpc` member.
- Existing mocked transport tests are updated so they no longer require or assert `jsonrpc: "2.0"` for Codex app-server messages.
- A regression test proves the Codex app-server stdio transport does not emit `Content-Length` frames.
- A malformed line or unsupported response shape produces an actionable infrastructure failure.
- Generic DevNexus MCP stdio behavior remains unchanged; this change is scoped to Codex app-server transport.

Boundaries:
- Do not add worker orchestration, MCP relay expansion, provider-native subagents, or Desktop automation in this slice.
- Do not read or write Codex private Desktop storage.
- Do not require a live Codex app-server for normal test runs.

Verification:
- Focused Codex app-server JSON-RPC/transport tests.
- Focused launch-provider tests that exercise the updated mock message shape.
- `npm run check` when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus main after verification passes under the configured bot publication policy.

items.title: Route Codex app-server notifications and server requests
items.description:
Type: implementation slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK) after dev-nexus:local-113
Source: docs/codex-architecture-design-audit-prd.md.

Goal:
Extend the Codex app-server client from single request/response handling into a bidirectional message loop that can route responses, server notifications, and server-initiated requests without losing early turn events or hanging approval requests.

User stories covered:
- As a coordinator, I can start a Codex app-server turn and receive terminal completion or failure facts from notifications rather than relying only on result-file polling.
- As a DevNexus user, provider-side approval or permission requests become visible infrastructure blockers instead of silent hangs.

Acceptance criteria:
- The app-server client has separate routing for request responses, notifications, and server requests.
- Notifications scoped to a turn can be buffered when they arrive before the caller registers a waiter.
- Server requests for approvals, tool/user input, or permission decisions are either surfaced through a supported DevNexus path or fail fast as explicit infrastructure blockers.
- Transport closure wakes pending response and notification waiters with clear errors.
- Tests cover early notification buffering, turn completion notification routing, server request rejection/blocking behavior, and transport failure cleanup.

Blockers and sequencing:
- Depends on dev-nexus:local-113 because routing should be built on the current Codex app-server JSONL/JSON-RPC-lite transport.

Boundaries:
- Do not implement provider-native subagent supervision.
- Do not store full transcript streams in DevNexus records.
- Do not require live Codex for normal tests.

Verification:
- Focused app-server client routing tests.
- Focused app-server launch tests.
- `npm run check` when feasible.

items.title: Add safe Codex app-server initialize probe and capability report
items.description:
Type: implementation slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK) after dev-nexus:local-113; can use dev-nexus:local-114 if the probe consumes notifications or server requests
Source: docs/codex-architecture-design-audit-prd.md.

Goal:
Add a safe readiness diagnostic that starts or connects to Codex app-server, calls initialize with a DevNexus client identity, reports available methods/capabilities, and exits without creating a durable user chat.

User stories covered:
- As a DevNexus user, I can check whether my installed Codex app-server supports the capabilities required by a configured profile.
- As a coordinator, I can distinguish auth, transport, protocol, missing-method, and policy blockers before dispatching worker turns.

Acceptance criteria:
- A CLI/MCP-accessible diagnostic reports Codex version when available, transport mode, endpoint scope, advertised methods, required capability status, optional capability status, and blocker summary.
- The probe does not submit user prompts and does not create visible durable worker chats.
- Capability groups include at least thread control, turn control, event stream readiness, MCP status, MCP tool call, schema generation, and remote-control disabled/blocked state.
- Non-loopback endpoints remain blocked unless host-local policy explicitly allows them.
- Tests use mocked app-server responses and cover all-good, missing required method, unsupported wire protocol, auth failure, and blocked non-loopback endpoint.

Blockers and sequencing:
- Depends on dev-nexus:local-113 for current wire compatibility.
- May depend on dev-nexus:local-114 if initialize or capability discovery needs bidirectional request handling.

Boundaries:
- Do not create Codex heartbeats or cron automations.
- Do not mutate Codex private storage.
- Do not make app-server mandatory for projects that use `codex exec`.

Verification:
- Focused capability diagnostic tests.
- `npm run check` when feasible.

items.title: Record provider session and turn facts for Codex app-server runs
items.description:
Type: implementation slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK) after dev-nexus:local-113 and preferably dev-nexus:local-114
Source: docs/codex-architecture-design-audit-prd.md.

Goal:
Make Codex app-server-backed launches observable through DevNexus records using provider-neutral session and turn facts, without storing full Codex transcripts or relying on Codex private storage.

User stories covered:
- As a maintainer, I can inspect a target report and see which provider thread and turn correspond to a failed or completed DevNexus run.
- As an automation operator, I can tell whether a Codex app-server run satisfied the DevNexus result contract or merely produced assistant text.

Acceptance criteria:
- Launch/run metadata can record provider id, executor mode, session id, turn id, purpose, component id, work item id, cwd/worktree id, profile id, model, reasoning effort, sandbox/approval summary, persistence mode, terminal status, and result-contract status where available.
- `target_report` summarizes recent app-server provider sessions and failures without reading provider-private chat storage.
- A completed provider turn without a valid DevNexus result contract remains failed or blocked.
- Event-derived completion/failure facts from dev-nexus:local-114, when available, reconcile with result-file facts without double-counting work.
- Tests cover completed, failed, interrupted/blocked, missing-result, and malformed-result app-server runs.

Blockers and sequencing:
- Depends on dev-nexus:local-113 for usable app-server communication.
- Should follow or coordinate with dev-nexus:local-114 for event-derived terminal status.

Boundaries:
- Do not archive full transcripts.
- Do not make DevNexus choose or supervise implementation work.
- Do not depend on Desktop-specific storage or UI state.

Verification:
- Focused launch/result/target-report tests.
- `npm run check` when feasible.

items.title: Slice hosting provisioning PRD into implementation work items
items.description:
Source PRD: docs/project-hosting-provisioning-prd.md

Turn the hosting provisioning PRD into independently verifiable implementation work items for DevNexus.

Expected slices should cover:
- hosting access declarations in project config validation/defaulting
- hosting status/plan/apply command and MCP surfaces
- provider-neutral planner actions and idempotent apply behavior
- GitHub adapter support for repository creation, collaborator permission repair, pending invitation detection, and invitee-profile invitation acceptance
- setup/status integration and documentation updates
- mocked provider tests before any live GitHub mutation path

Boundary: do not manually repair collaborators as part of this item. The goal is to slice the declared DevNexus tooling work, keeping the user-facing API dumb and obvious while the hosting tool owns provider complexity.

items.title: Add hosting access declarations and provisioning gates
items.description:
Type: implementation tracer-bullet slice
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK)
Parent planning item: dev-nexus:local-117
Source PRD: docs/project-hosting-provisioning-prd.md

Goal: extend DevNexus hosting configuration so projects can declare required repository access and explicit provisioning gates with a minimal, obvious API.

User stories covered:
- As a project owner, I can declare that a meta-project repository should exist under a bot account and grant my human account access.
- As a maintainer reviewing project configuration, I can tell the intended repository access model by reading a short configuration block.

Acceptance criteria:
- Project config validates hosting access principals with kind, provider identity, intended role, required permission, optional auth profile, and invitation policy.
- Provisioning config supports explicit gates for repository creation, local remote repair, access repair, invitation acceptance, default-branch repair, and visibility repair.
- Existing hosting configs without access declarations remain valid through defaults or compatibility normalization.
- Invalid duplicate principals, unsupported permission values, unknown principal kinds, and malformed policy values fail with actionable errors.
- Shared project config does not store tokens, private keys, GitHub CLI state, or browser/session material.

Boundaries:
- Do not add provider mutation in this slice.
- Do not contact GitHub or other live providers in tests.
- Do not make GitHub mandatory for all DevNexus projects.

Verification:
- Focused project config tests for old configs, minimal access declarations, multi-principal access, invalid values, and provisioning defaults.
- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus main after verification passes under dogfood policy.

items.title: Build read-only hosting status model
items.description:
Type: implementation tracer-bullet slice
Readiness: todo/dependent on dev-nexus:local-118
Parent planning item: dev-nexus:local-117
Source PRD: docs/project-hosting-provisioning-prd.md

Goal: build a read-only hosting status model that answers what is true right now without mutating local Git remotes or provider state.

User stories covered:
- As a coordinator agent, I can report missing repository access as a setup blocker instead of inventing provider commands.
- As a new host joining a project, I can validate local human and bot auth profiles against shared hosting intent.

Acceptance criteria:
- Hosting status reports configured provider, namespace, resolved repository name, repository existence, visibility, default branch, expected remotes, local remote matches, auth profiles, observed actors, required access, pending invitations, warnings, and blockers.
- Status distinguishes missing hosting config, missing auth profiles, wrong observed actor, provider mismatch, repository missing, access missing, insufficient access, and pending invitation states.
- Status never creates repositories, invites collaborators, accepts invitations, updates remotes, or writes provider state.
- Provider reads are abstracted so tests can use mocked provider responses.
- Output is structured enough for later CLI/MCP presentation and setup checks.

Dependencies:
- Depends on dev-nexus:local-118 hosting access declarations and provisioning gates.

Boundaries:
- Do not implement apply/mutation behavior in this slice.
- Do not run live GitHub calls in default tests.

Verification:
- Focused hosting status tests for healthy, missing repository, missing local remote, wrong remote URL, missing auth profile, wrong actor, missing access, insufficient access, pending invite, and unsupported provider cases.
- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus main after verification passes under dogfood policy.

items.title: Build deterministic hosting plan actions
items.description:
Type: implementation tracer-bullet slice
Readiness: todo/dependent on dev-nexus:local-118 and dev-nexus:local-119
Parent planning item: dev-nexus:local-117
Source PRD: docs/project-hosting-provisioning-prd.md

Goal: turn hosting status into deterministic dry-run plan actions that explain what DevNexus would do and what remains blocked.

User stories covered:
- As a project owner, I can run a dry-run and see exactly what exists, what is missing, and what would change.
- As a coordinator agent, I can paste a stable provisioning plan into a work-item comment.

Acceptance criteria:
- Planner emits ordered structured actions for repository creation, local remote add/update, collaborator add, collaborator permission update, collaborator invite, pending invitation acceptance, wait/manual invitation, default branch repair, visibility mismatch, and unsupported provider operations.
- Every action includes provider, target repository, target principal or remote, current state, desired state, mutation class, allowed/blocked/manual state, reason, and auth profile that would perform the action.
- Plans are stable across repeated runs with identical status input.
- Planner honors provisioning gates from project configuration and never performs mutations.
- Unsafe visibility changes and unsupported provider operations are declined with actionable reasons.

Dependencies:
- Depends on dev-nexus:local-118 and dev-nexus:local-119.

Boundaries:
- Do not implement apply execution in this slice.
- Do not contact live providers outside the read-only status inputs already modeled.

Verification:
- Focused planner snapshot or structural tests for each action class, blocked gate, manual wait state, action ordering, and no-mutation behavior.
- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus main after verification passes under dogfood policy.

items.title: Expose project hosting status and plan in CLI and MCP
items.description:
Type: user-surface implementation slice
Readiness: todo/dependent on dev-nexus:local-119 and dev-nexus:local-120
Parent planning item: dev-nexus:local-117
Source PRD: docs/project-hosting-provisioning-prd.md

Goal: expose the read-only hosting status and plan through small, obvious CLI and MCP surfaces.

User stories covered:
- As a coordinator agent, I can ask DevNexus for hosting facts without provider-specific tools.
- As a project owner, I can run one command to see current hosting state and one command to see planned repairs.

Acceptance criteria:
- CLI supports `dev-nexus project hosting status <project-root> [--json]` and `dev-nexus project hosting plan <project-root> [--json]` or an equivalently obvious command shape that fits existing CLI conventions.
- MCP exposes matching structured tools for hosting status and plan.
- Text output is compact and readable; JSON output preserves full structured status and plan details.
- Missing hosting config, missing auth profiles, missing provider support, and blocked actions produce non-crashing actionable output.
- Status and plan commands do not mutate local Git remotes or provider state.

Dependencies:
- Depends on dev-nexus:local-119 and dev-nexus:local-120.

Boundaries:
- Do not expose apply/mutation commands in this slice.
- Do not require live GitHub credentials for normal unit tests.

Verification:
- Focused CLI and MCP tests for status, plan, JSON/text output, missing config, missing auth profile, and blocked plan action cases.
- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus main after verification passes under dogfood policy.

items.title: Add local remote repair hosting apply
items.description:
Type: implementation tracer-bullet slice
Readiness: todo/dependent on dev-nexus:local-120 and dev-nexus:local-121
Parent planning item: dev-nexus:local-117
Source PRD: docs/project-hosting-provisioning-prd.md

Goal: implement the first safe apply path by repairing only declared local Git remotes through the hosting plan.

User stories covered:
- As an automation account, I can configure the declared remotes without accidentally using the human account.
- As a maintainer, I can run apply twice against a healthy project without churn.

Acceptance criteria:
- `project hosting apply` or equivalent apply service recomputes the plan immediately before mutation.
- Local remote add/update actions execute only when remote repair is explicitly allowed by project provisioning policy.
- Apply mutates only declared remote names and preserves undeclared remotes.
- Apply refuses blocked/manual actions and reports skipped actions clearly.
- Apply returns a final status or plan summary after mutation.
- Running apply twice against already-correct remotes performs no further mutations.

Dependencies:
- Depends on dev-nexus:local-120 and dev-nexus:local-121.

Boundaries:
- Do not create repositories or call live provider mutation APIs in this slice.
- Do not delete local remotes.
- Do not mutate unknown remotes.

Verification:
- Focused tests for add remote, update remote, preserve undeclared remote, blocked gate, idempotent rerun, and final status reporting.
- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus main after verification passes under dogfood policy.

items.title: Add GitHub repository creation hosting apply
items.description:
Type: provider-mutation implementation slice
Readiness: todo/dependent on dev-nexus:local-120 and dev-nexus:local-121
Parent planning item: dev-nexus:local-117
Source PRD: docs/project-hosting-provisioning-prd.md

Goal: implement policy-gated GitHub repository creation behind the provider adapter and hosting apply plan.

User stories covered:
- As a project owner, I can declare that a private meta-project repository should exist under the automation namespace.
- As an automation account, DevNexus can create the repository only when project policy explicitly allows it.

Acceptance criteria:
- GitHub provider adapter can create a repository under a user or organization namespace in mocked tests.
- Apply executes repository creation only when the recomputed plan marks it allowed and repository creation is explicitly enabled.
- Creation uses declared visibility and default branch intent where supported, and reports provider URL and remote URL facts after mutation.
- Missing auth profile, wrong actor, insufficient token scope, organization policy blocks, namespace mismatch, and already-existing repository states produce actionable results.
- Re-running apply after creation is idempotent and does not attempt duplicate creation.

Dependencies:
- Depends on dev-nexus:local-120 and dev-nexus:local-121.

Boundaries:
- Do not implement collaborator repair or invitation acceptance in this slice.
- Do not run live GitHub mutation in default tests.
- Do not create organizations or provider accounts.

Verification:
- Mocked GitHub provider tests for create success, already exists, insufficient permission, namespace policy rejection, missing auth profile, and idempotent rerun.
- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus main after verification passes under dogfood policy.

items.title: Add GitHub collaborator repair and pending invite detection
items.description:
Type: provider-mutation implementation slice
Readiness: todo/dependent on dev-nexus:local-118 through dev-nexus:local-121
Parent planning item: dev-nexus:local-117
Source PRD: docs/project-hosting-provisioning-prd.md

Goal: make GitHub access drift explicit and repair declared collaborators when provisioning policy allows it.

User stories covered:
- As a project owner, I can declare required human and automation access without spelling out GitHub collaborator commands.
- As a coordinator agent, I can report missing or insufficient account access as hosting drift.

Acceptance criteria:
- GitHub provider adapter can inspect effective collaborator permission and pending collaborator invitation state in mocked tests.
- Hosting status and plan distinguish missing collaborator, insufficient permission, pending invitation, provider policy block, and unknown access states.
- Apply adds or updates only declared collaborators when access repair is explicitly allowed.
- Apply does not grant broad access to undeclared accounts or teams.
- Pending invitations are reported separately from completed effective access.
- Organization policy, single sign-on, missing scope, disabled collaborator invites, and unsupported principal kinds produce actionable blockers.

Dependencies:
- Depends on dev-nexus:local-118, dev-nexus:local-119, dev-nexus:local-120, and dev-nexus:local-121.

Boundaries:
- Do not accept invitations as the invitee in this slice.
- Do not create teams or manage organization membership.
- Do not run live GitHub mutation in default tests.

Verification:
- Mocked GitHub provider tests for missing collaborator, insufficient permission, successful invite/add/update, pending invitation, organization policy rejection, and idempotent rerun.
- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus main after verification passes under dogfood policy.

items.title: Accept GitHub invitations through invitee auth profiles
items.description:
Type: provider-mutation implementation slice
Readiness: todo/dependent on dev-nexus:local-124
Parent planning item: dev-nexus:local-117
Source PRD: docs/project-hosting-provisioning-prd.md

Goal: let DevNexus accept a pending GitHub repository invitation automatically when the invited principal has a configured auth profile and project policy allows it.

User stories covered:
- As an invited maintainer, I can let DevNexus accept a pending provider invitation for me when my host-local auth profile is configured.
- As a human maintainer, I can see when an invitation is pending and whether DevNexus can complete acceptance without browser/manual steps.

Acceptance criteria:
- GitHub provider adapter can list repository invitations for the authenticated invited user in mocked tests.
- Apply accepts a pending repository invitation as the invited principal only when invitation acceptance is explicitly allowed and the invitee auth profile is available.
- DevNexus verifies the invitee auth profile is authenticated as the expected provider login before accepting.
- Pending invitation is not treated as completed access until successful acceptance and final status confirms effective access.
- Missing invitee auth profile, wrong account, expired invitation, insufficient token scope, provider policy rejection, and invitation not found produce distinct actionable blockers.
- Re-running apply after acceptance is idempotent.

Dependencies:
- Depends on dev-nexus:local-124.

Boundaries:
- Do not require browser-based invitation acceptance.
- Do not accept invitations for undeclared principals.
- Do not run live GitHub mutation in default tests.

Verification:
- Mocked GitHub provider tests for successful acceptance, missing profile, wrong account, expired invitation, insufficient scope, provider rejection, no pending invitation, and final effective-access confirmation.
- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus main after verification passes under dogfood policy.

items.title: Integrate hosting status into setup and documentation
items.description:
Type: setup and documentation slice
Readiness: todo/dependent on dev-nexus:local-121, with follow-up refresh after apply slices land
Parent planning item: dev-nexus:local-117
Source PRD: docs/project-hosting-provisioning-prd.md

Goal: replace ad hoc setup guidance with DevNexus hosting status and plan output, and document the minimal API.

User stories covered:
- As a new host joining a project, I can validate that host-local human and bot auth profiles match shared hosting intent before running automation.
- As a maintainer, I can understand that provider-specific mechanics belong in the tool, not in project config or agent chat memory.

Acceptance criteria:
- Setup checks consume hosting status and plan instead of telling users to manually run provider-specific commands for normal facts DevNexus can inspect.
- Setup/status output reports repository, remote, auth profile, actor, access, and pending invitation drift concisely.
- Documentation shows minimal hosting config examples for repository, remotes, required access, and provisioning gates.
- Documentation explains that provider details, collaborator invitations, and invitation acceptance are handled by hosting adapters.
- Documentation states that secrets and local credential material stay in host-local config, not shared project config.
- After apply slices land, setup docs are updated to describe allowed repair and invitation acceptance behavior.

Dependencies:
- Depends on dev-nexus:local-121 for read-only status/plan surfaces; should be revisited after dev-nexus:local-122 through dev-nexus:local-125.

Boundaries:
- Do not run live GitHub mutation from setup checks.
- Do not include personal paths, tokens, or account-specific dogfood-only details in generic docs.

Verification:
- Focused setup assistant tests for hosting status integration, missing auth profile, pending invitation, blocked plan action, and no-provider-mutation behavior.
- Static documentation review.
- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus main after verification passes under dogfood policy.

items.title: Add opt-in fake-project hosting integration test
items.description:
Type: opt-in integration testing slice
Readiness: todo/dependent on mocked provider apply support in dev-nexus:local-123 through dev-nexus:local-125
Parent planning item: dev-nexus:local-117
Source PRD: docs/project-hosting-provisioning-prd.md

Goal: validate live GitHub hosting provisioning against a disposable fake DevNexus project/repository fixture, not against `dev-nexus-plexus` or any other active project.

User stories covered:
- As a maintainer, I can prove repository creation, collaborator repair, and invitation acceptance work end to end without risking an active project.
- As an agent, I can run normal tests without live GitHub mutation unless the integration test is explicitly enabled.

Acceptance criteria:
- Integration test fixture builds a disposable fake DevNexus project config with a unique repository name, declared remotes, required access, and provisioning gates.
- Live GitHub integration test is opt-in only through explicit test configuration or environment, and is skipped by default in normal `npm run check`.
- The test creates or connects only the disposable fake repository, applies allowed hosting repairs, validates final status, and records enough cleanup information for safe teardown.
- The test does not depend on `dev-nexus-plexus`, dogfood project state, or any active project repository.
- Cleanup behavior avoids deleting or mutating non-fixture repositories and refuses to proceed when the repository name/owner does not match the disposable fixture policy.
- Missing live credentials, missing invitee profile, wrong actor, provider policy block, and cleanup refusal produce clear skipped or blocked results rather than destructive fallback.

Dependencies:
- Depends on dev-nexus:local-123, dev-nexus:local-124, and dev-nexus:local-125.

Boundaries:
- Do not run live GitHub mutation in default tests.
- Do not use active DevNexus projects as integration fixtures.
- Do not delete arbitrary repositories or broaden cleanup outside the disposable fixture naming policy.

Verification:
- Mocked tests for fixture planning, opt-in gating, wrong-owner refusal, cleanup refusal, and skipped default behavior.
- Optional live GitHub integration run only when explicitly configured.
- npm run check must pass without live credentials.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus main after mocked verification passes; live integration evidence can be recorded separately when explicitly run.

items.title: Define cross-tracker discovery and inbound issue sync PRD
items.description:
Type: Product Requirements Document (PRD) planning slice
Readiness: ready for PRD attachment, then issue slicing

Goal: capture the product behavior for DevNexus issue discovery across configured work trackers, including whether GitHub Issues created on one host become visible to workers on another host without manual prompting.

Context:
- Current eligible-work and coordinator selection use component default work tracking, not every configured tracker.
- Current work-item sync execution supports local source trackers to GitHub target trackers, not GitHub inbound import into local.
- Cross-machine workers only see local tracker additions after the meta-project repository is synchronized.

Acceptance criteria:
- PRD states present behavior accurately.
- PRD defines desired tracker roles, discovery aggregation, deduplication, inbound import, and cross-host visibility behavior.
- PRD separates read-only discovery from mutating sync/import operations.
- PRD calls out permission, credential, conflict, and fail-closed behavior.
- PRD is attached to this work item and ready for issue slicing.

Verification:
- Static document review.
- JSON tracker store parses.

items.title: Add tracker discovery roles and policy defaults
items.description:
Type: implementation tracer-bullet slice
Readiness: ready
Parent planning item: dev-nexus:local-128
Source PRD: docs/tracker-discovery-inbound-sync-prd.md

Goal: extend the existing multi-tracker configuration model so projects can declare tracker discovery roles and default discovery policy without changing current default-tracker behavior.

User stories covered:
- As a maintainer, I can declare which trackers are primary, eligible sources, external inboxes, mirrors, coordination trackers, or archives.
- As a coordinator agent, I can inspect discovery policy without inferring behavior from provider type.

Acceptance criteria:
- Tracker bindings can declare explicit roles such as primary, eligible_source, external_inbox, mirror, coordination, and archive.
- Component discovery policy can declare scanned roles, whether direct external selection is allowed, whether import is required first, provider filters, query limits, conflict winner policy, and missing-credential behavior.
- Existing project configs continue to behave as default-tracker-only when no discovery policy is declared.
- Invalid roles and invalid discovery-policy combinations produce clear validation errors.
- Normalized component status exposes tracker roles and the effective default discovery policy compactly.

Boundaries:
- Do not implement provider reads, eligible-work aggregation, or import execution in this slice.
- Do not store host-local credentials or auth paths in shared project config.

Verification:
- Focused tests for role normalization, default policy preservation, invalid role rejection, invalid policy rejection, and status normalization.
- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus main after verification passes under dogfood policy.

items.title: Report read-only tracker discovery status
items.description:
Type: read-only status implementation slice
Readiness: todo/dependent on dev-nexus:local-129
Parent planning item: dev-nexus:local-128
Source PRD: docs/tracker-discovery-inbound-sync-prd.md

Goal: add a read-only discovery status surface that tells maintainers and agents exactly which configured trackers are visible, eligible, optional, blocked, or import-only.

User stories covered:
- As a maintainer, I can ask whether a GitHub inbox will be ignored, listed directly, or imported before selection.
- As a worker on another host, I can see whether provider credentials are required for discovery and whether missing access is a warning or blocker.

Acceptance criteria:
- DevNexus exposes a read-only discovery status service and a small CLI/MCP surface such as work_item_discovery_status or an equivalent naming convention that fits the codebase.
- Output includes each component's default tracker, configured tracker bindings, roles, effective discovery policy, provider capability report, and whether each tracker is readable by the current actor.
- Missing optional credentials are warnings or skipped trackers according to policy; required credentials are blockers.
- The command never mutates local tracker files, Git state, provider issues, or link records.
- Mocked local and GitHub tracker cases cover readable, missing credentials, unsupported capability, disabled tracker, and policy-blocked tracker states.

Dependencies:
- Depends on dev-nexus:local-129.

Boundaries:
- Do not aggregate eligible work or create local imported items in this slice.
- Do not require live GitHub credentials for default tests.

Verification:
- Focused service, CLI, and MCP tests for status output, JSON/text shape, provider capability reporting, optional/required credential behavior, and no-mutation behavior.
- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus main after verification passes under dogfood policy.

items.title: Aggregate eligible work from configured discovery sources
items.description:
Type: eligible-work implementation slice
Readiness: todo/dependent on dev-nexus:local-129 and dev-nexus:local-130
Parent planning item: dev-nexus:local-128
Source PRD: docs/tracker-discovery-inbound-sync-prd.md

Goal: let eligible-work and coordinator surfaces opt into scanning configured discovery sources while preserving current default-tracker behavior by default.

User stories covered:
- As a coordinator agent, I can list eligible work across configured discovery sources when policy asks for it.
- As a maintainer, I can keep default-tracker-only selection unless the project explicitly opts into discovery mode.

Acceptance criteria:
- Default eligible-work mode remains behaviorally unchanged and lists the component default tracker only.
- Discovery mode queries trackers whose roles and policy mark them as discovery sources, applying configured statuses, labels, milestones, assignees, provider query filters, per-tracker limits, and final limits.
- Returned items include component id, logical item id when known, canonical assignment tracker reference, discovered source tracker reference, warnings, and whether the item is selectable or import-only.
- When policy requires import first, unimported external items are reported as import candidates and are not assigned as local work.
- Missing provider access, disabled trackers, unsupported list capability, and provider read errors are reported according to discovery policy without crashing the whole eligible-work call.

Dependencies:
- Depends on dev-nexus:local-129 and dev-nexus:local-130.

Boundaries:
- Do not implement inbound import mutation in this slice.
- Do not make every configured tracker an eligible source by default.

Verification:
- Focused tests for default mode preservation, opt-in discovery aggregation, per-tracker filters, import-only candidates, provider warnings/blockers, and final ordering/limits.
- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus main after verification passes under dogfood policy.

items.title: Deduplicate linked work items across trackers
items.description:
Type: logical-identity implementation slice
Readiness: todo/dependent on dev-nexus:local-129; can be implemented before or with dev-nexus:local-131
Parent planning item: dev-nexus:local-128
Source PRD: docs/tracker-discovery-inbound-sync-prd.md

Goal: prevent eligible-work discovery from returning duplicate assignments when the same logical work item exists in local and external trackers.

User stories covered:
- As a worker agent, I receive one canonical assignment, not separate local and GitHub copies of the same work.
- As a migration owner, I can see ambiguous or stale tracker links instead of DevNexus silently merging unrelated issues.

Acceptance criteria:
- Deduplication uses existing tracker links, provider external references on local items, stable provider repository/item number or key, and optional configured fingerprints where available.
- Linked local and external items collapse to one canonical item according to discovery policy, with a deduplication reason or link id in the result.
- Stale links, missing target items, conflicting links, and ambiguous unlinked matches are reported as warnings or blockers according to policy.
- DevNexus does not silently collapse items when identity cannot be proven.
- The dedupe result can be reused by eligible-work aggregation and inbound import planning.

Dependencies:
- Depends on dev-nexus:local-129.
- Coordinates with dev-nexus:local-131 for eligible-work integration.

Boundaries:
- Do not mutate link records or tracker items in this slice.
- Do not implement provider write-back or inbound import execution.

Verification:
- Focused tests for linked duplicates, externalRef duplicates, repository/number matching, stale links, ambiguous matches, and canonical source selection.
- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus main after verification passes under dogfood policy.

items.title: Plan inbound GitHub-to-local issue import
items.description:
Type: dry-run inbound import implementation slice
Readiness: todo/dependent on dev-nexus:local-129 and dev-nexus:local-132
Parent planning item: dev-nexus:local-128
Source PRD: docs/tracker-discovery-inbound-sync-prd.md

Goal: add a read-only inbound import plan from a configured external tracker such as GitHub Issues into the canonical local tracker.

User stories covered:
- As a maintainer, I can dry-run GitHub-to-local import and review creates, updates, skips, conflicts, and missing links before any local files change.
- As a Windows worker, I can know which provider-native issues would become local work once import is executed.

Acceptance criteria:
- DevNexus exposes a small planning surface such as work_item_inbound_sync_plan or an equivalent command shape that fits existing sync conventions.
- The plan supports external-to-local direction starting with GitHub source and local target trackers.
- Planning reports creates, updates, skips, conflicts, ambiguous duplicates, stale links, missing credentials, unsupported capabilities, provider filters, field/status mappings, and exact local files that would change.
- Planning is idempotent: linked items and configured fingerprints map back to the same local logical item on repeated runs.
- The plan is strictly read-only and does not create local items, update link records, comment on provider issues, or mutate Git state.

Dependencies:
- Depends on dev-nexus:local-129 and dev-nexus:local-132.

Boundaries:
- Do not implement apply/execution in this slice.
- Do not add provider-native writes or comments in this slice.
- Do not require live GitHub credentials for default tests.

Verification:
- Focused tests for create/update/skip/conflict plans, ambiguous duplicate plans, stale link plans, missing credential behavior, exact local-file reporting, and no-mutation behavior with mocked GitHub provider data.
- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus main after verification passes under dogfood policy.

items.title: Execute policy-gated inbound import to local tracker
items.description:
Type: guarded mutation implementation slice
Readiness: todo/dependent on dev-nexus:local-98 and dev-nexus:local-133
Parent planning item: dev-nexus:local-128
Source PRD: docs/tracker-discovery-inbound-sync-prd.md

Goal: execute a reviewed inbound import plan by creating or updating canonical local work items and tracker links from external provider issues, with fail-closed mutation safety.

User stories covered:
- As a project owner, I can keep local work items canonical while importing selected GitHub Issues.
- As a bot account, I can read a GitHub inbox and write local tracker state only when authority, policy, and checkout safety allow it.

Acceptance criteria:
- Execution requires explicit source and target tracker ids, external-to-local direction, a write policy allowing local tracker mutation, and effective actor authority for local work-item writes and provider reads.
- Execution recomputes or validates the import plan immediately before mutation and refuses blocked, stale, or ambiguous plans.
- Execution creates or updates local canonical work items and link records idempotently; rerunning against the same provider items does not create duplicates.
- Execution writes only local tracker/link files for the owning DevNexus project and does not write provider issues in the first implementation.
- Execution fails closed in shared checkouts unless the path is classified as an owned project-meta worktree or explicit integration context.
- Execution records a concise import summary through the work-item service or run result without leaking secrets.

Dependencies:
- Depends on dev-nexus:local-98 for shared-checkout mutation enforcement.
- Depends on dev-nexus:local-133 for inbound import planning.

Boundaries:
- Do not implement provider-native issue comments or provider writes in this slice.
- Do not run live GitHub mutation in default tests.
- Do not import from unconfigured repositories.

Verification:
- Focused tests for allowed local import, idempotent rerun, blocked policy, missing authority, shared-checkout refusal, stale plan refusal, ambiguous conflict refusal, and no provider writes.
- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus main after verification passes under dogfood policy.

items.title: Summarize external issue visibility in agent and target surfaces
items.description:
Type: agent-facing reporting slice
Readiness: todo/dependent on dev-nexus:local-130 and dev-nexus:local-131; refresh after dev-nexus:local-133 when import planning lands
Parent planning item: dev-nexus:local-128
Source PRD: docs/tracker-discovery-inbound-sync-prd.md

Goal: make external issue visibility explicit in automation status, eligible-work output, agent launch context, and target reports.

User stories covered:
- As a coordinator agent, I can answer whether provider-native GitHub Issues are ignored, directly selectable, or import candidates.
- As a cross-host worker, I can tell whether I need to fetch the meta-project, query a provider, run import, or wait for scheduled sync.

Acceptance criteria:
- Automation status and eligible-work summaries include concise discovery mode, configured source roles, selected/ignored/import-only tracker counts, and provider access warnings.
- Agent launch context states whether external issues are automatically visible, import-required, direct-selectable, or explicitly ignored for each component.
- Target report includes last discovery/import status when available without duplicating long provider payloads.
- The summaries remain compact enough for coordinator prompts and do not leak tokens, secrets, or host-local auth paths.
- Default-tracker-only projects continue to produce simple output without noisy empty discovery sections.

Dependencies:
- Depends on dev-nexus:local-130 and dev-nexus:local-131.
- Should be refreshed after dev-nexus:local-133 adds import planning facts.

Boundaries:
- Do not implement new provider reads or import execution in this slice.
- Do not make summaries the source of truth for tracker policy.

Verification:
- Focused tests for automation status, eligible-work, agent context, and target report summaries in default-only, discovery, import-required, missing-credential, and no-eligible-work cases.
- npm run check when feasible.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus main after verification passes under dogfood policy.

items.title: Add fake GitHub inbox discovery and import smoke test
items.description:
Type: opt-in integration testing slice
Readiness: todo/dependent on dev-nexus:local-133 and dev-nexus:local-134
Parent planning item: dev-nexus:local-128
Source PRD: docs/tracker-discovery-inbound-sync-prd.md

Goal: prove the external-inbox path against a fake or disposable GitHub-like source, not against an active DevNexus project.

User stories covered:
- As a maintainer, I can validate that a provider-native issue can become a canonical local work item without risking active project state.
- As an agent, I can run normal checks without live provider mutation or hidden dependency on another project.

Acceptance criteria:
- Test coverage uses a mocked provider or disposable fake-project fixture that represents a configured GitHub issue inbox and canonical local tracker.
- The smoke proves discovery status, import planning, and guarded import execution can take a provider-native issue to a canonical local item and link record.
- Default verification does not require live GitHub credentials and does not mutate live provider issues.
- Optional live validation, if added, is opt-in only and constrained to a disposable repository/project fixture with strict naming and cleanup policy.
- The test does not depend on dev-nexus-plexus, this dogfood project as a live provider target, or any other active project.
- Missing live credentials, wrong actor, provider policy block, and cleanup refusal produce skipped or blocked results rather than unsafe fallback.

Dependencies:
- Depends on dev-nexus:local-133 for import planning.
- Depends on dev-nexus:local-134 for guarded import execution.

Boundaries:
- Do not use active DevNexus projects as integration fixtures.
- Do not run live provider mutation in default tests.
- Do not delete arbitrary repositories or issues.

Verification:
- Mocked smoke tests for discovery-to-plan-to-import, duplicate rerun, missing credentials, wrong source filter, and no-live-provider default behavior.
- Optional live smoke only when explicitly configured.
- npm run check must pass without live credentials.

Publication notes:
- Direct integration to DevNexus main after mocked verification passes; live integration evidence can be recorded separately when explicitly run.

items.title: Decide dogfood external issue selection policy
items.description:
Type: human-in-the-loop decision slice
Readiness: blocked on product/authority decision
Parent planning item: dev-nexus:local-128
Source PRD: docs/tracker-discovery-inbound-sync-prd.md

Goal: record the dogfood policy choices that should not be guessed by an implementation agent.

Questions to decide:
- Should dogfood ever allow direct assignment of external GitHub Issues, or should every external issue become a local canonical item first?
- Should imported GitHub Issues receive a provider comment linking back to the local DevNexus item, or should the first inbound implementation avoid provider writes entirely?
- Should scheduled automation run inbound import before every coordinator cycle, or should import remain a separate explicit step until authority and mutation guards are fully landed?
- How should imported external issues be ordered relative to locally authored ready items when both match the automation selector?

Acceptance criteria:
- The selected dogfood policy is recorded in project documentation or target state.
- Implementation issues that need the decision are updated or unblocked with the chosen policy.
- The decision states whether provider writes are forbidden, optional, or explicitly gated for the first inbound path.
- The decision states whether inbound import is manually invoked or scheduler-integrated during the first rollout.

Boundaries:
- Do not implement code in this item.
- Do not grant broader provider write authority as part of the decision record.

Verification:
- Static review of the recorded decision and affected work items.

Publication notes:
- Meta-project update only, using the configured bot/automation profile when published.

items.title: Fix Mac setup assistant path rendering and source-root fallback
items.description:
Type: Mac setup regression fix
Readiness: ready
Source evidence: Mac sync/test run on 2026-05-18 after syncing DevNexus to 37717f7.

Observed failure:
- Command: npm run check
- Host: macOS 26.4.1, Node v26.0.0, npm 11.12.1, git 2.54.0
- Package: @evref-bl/dev-nexus@0.1.0-alpha.16
- Failing file: src/nexusSetupAssistant.test.ts
- Full result: 1 failed test file, 8 failed tests, 56 passed test files, 450 passed tests, 1 skipped.

Representative failures:
- Mac setup plan expected clone commands containing $HOME/dev-nexus and $HOME/dev-nexus/mac-demo, but generated commands did not include those host-friendly Mac paths.
- Mac setup with a Windows sourceRoot expected fallback to $HOME/dev-nexus/mac-demo/components/dev-nexus, but generated commands used a /var/folders/... temporary project path.
- Setup check with an existing component directory expected component-dev-nexus-git-checkout to report a non-Git checkout, but the check reported a missing source root under a Windows-style $env:USERPROFILE path.

Goal: make join-existing-project setup path resolution deterministic and platform-correct on macOS while preserving Windows behavior.

Acceptance criteria:
- buildNexusSetupPlan({ platform: "macos" }) renders Mac user-facing setup commands with $HOME-style paths where the tests expect portable Mac guidance.
- A Windows-only sourceRoot in project config is ignored for Mac setup commands and falls back to the Mac project-local component checkout path.
- Missing sourceRoot falls back to the Mac project-local component checkout path rather than the temporary fixture root or a Windows path.
- buildNexusSetupCheck inspects an existing component directory at the resolved platform-appropriate component path and reports not-a-Git-checkout, wrong remote, and dirty-state cases correctly.
- The failing Mac path/source-root tests in src/nexusSetupAssistant.test.ts pass on this Mac.

Boundaries:
- Do not weaken setup safety checks or hide genuinely missing component checkouts.
- Do not encode this developer's absolute /Users path in generic setup output.

Verification:
- npm test -- src/nexusSetupAssistant.test.ts
- npm run check on macOS.

items.title: Keep setup assistant advisory checks from being masked by component path failures
items.description:
Type: setup-check status classification fix
Readiness: todo/dependent on dev-nexus:local-138 unless investigation proves an independent root cause
Source evidence: Mac sync/test run on 2026-05-18 after syncing DevNexus to 37717f7.

Observed failure:
- Command: npm run check
- Host: macOS 26.4.1, Node v26.0.0, npm 11.12.1, git 2.54.0
- Package: @evref-bl/dev-nexus@0.1.0-alpha.16
- Failing file: src/nexusSetupAssistant.test.ts

Specific failed expectations:
- uses configured agent MCP targets in setup guidance and checks: expected overall setup check status warning, received blocked.
- warns when plugin-projected skills and MCP servers are not materialized: expected warning, received blocked.
- warns until agent MCP visibility is recorded for the host: expected warning, received blocked.

Likely relationship to local-138:
- These tests are intended to assert advisory missing-agent-session or missing-projection warnings.
- On this Mac run, setup status becomes blocked because component source-root resolution reports a blocking missing checkout before those advisory checks can determine the overall status.

Goal: keep setup assistant advisory checks observable and warning-classified when component checkout fixtures are present, instead of letting platform path resolution or unrelated component checks mask them.

Acceptance criteria:
- Agent MCP target checks, plugin projection checks, and agent project-session checks remain warning-classified when their only problem is missing materialization or unrecorded visibility.
- Test fixtures that create a component checkout are recognized by setup checks on macOS and Windows.
- Blocking component checkout problems still make the overall status blocked when they are genuinely present.
- The three advisory-status failures in src/nexusSetupAssistant.test.ts pass on this Mac.

Dependencies:
- Depends on dev-nexus:local-138 if the root cause is the same platform path resolver.

Verification:
- npm test -- src/nexusSetupAssistant.test.ts
- npm run check on macOS.

items.title: Suppress Git default-branch warning noise in setup assistant tests
items.description:
Type: test hygiene and toolchain-friction fix
Readiness: todo
Source evidence: Mac sync/test run on 2026-05-18 after syncing DevNexus to 37717f7.

Observed friction:
- Command: npm run check
- Host: macOS 26.4.1, Node v26.0.0, npm 11.12.1, git 2.54.0
- During src/nexusSetupAssistant.test.ts, multiple tests that run git init emit Git's default-branch warning: "Using 'master' as the name for the initial branch. This default branch name will change to 'main' in Git 3.0..."

Goal: keep setup assistant test output quiet and deterministic across Git versions and host defaults.

Acceptance criteria:
- Test helpers that create temporary Git repositories specify an initial branch or otherwise suppress the Git default-branch advice in a local, test-scoped way.
- The tests do not require changing the developer's global Git config.
- The generated temporary repositories still exercise the same setup-check behavior.
- npm test -- src/nexusSetupAssistant.test.ts no longer prints repeated default-branch warning blocks on this Mac.

Boundaries:
- Do not hide stderr globally for commands whose failures should remain diagnosable.
- Do not require a specific global init.defaultBranch setting.

Verification:
- npm test -- src/nexusSetupAssistant.test.ts
- npm run check on macOS.

items.title: Make project create/import scaffold failures recoverable
items.description:
Type: project lifecycle reliability defect
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK)
Source: dedicated MCP-Pharo project setup on 2026-05-18.

Observed failure:
A DevNexus-Pharo scaffold error during project import left a partially initialized project root containing `.git`, `dev-nexus.project.json`, and `plexus.project.json`, but no completed AGENTS setup and no usable registry entry. Manual cleanup/repair and re-import were required.

Current evidence:
- `src/nexusProjectOperations.ts` writes the project root, initializes Git, saves `dev-nexus.project.json`, and then calls `scaffoldNexusProject(...)`.
- If an extension `installProjectFiles` hook throws, the root can remain ambiguous: not empty, not fully registered, and not clearly resumable.

Goal:
Make create/import failures during scaffold extension setup atomic enough that users do not need manual repair, or explicitly resumable with a first-class repair/retry path.

Acceptance criteria:
- A throwing scaffold extension during project create does not leave an ambiguous partial project root, or leaves a documented resumable state with a clear repair/retry command.
- A throwing scaffold extension during project import has the same recoverable behavior and does not register a completed project reference.
- Existing source checkout imports remain non-destructive; rollback must not delete or mutate the imported source checkout.
- Error output names the failed extension/scaffold phase and the safe next action.
- Tests cover create and import with a scaffold extension that throws after initial files would otherwise have been written.

Boundaries:
- Keep plugin-specific template behavior out of DevNexus core.
- Do not make DevNexus choose or supervise implementation work; this is only project lifecycle robustness.

Verification:
- Focused `nexusProjectOperations` create/import failure tests.
- `npm run check` in DevNexus when feasible.

items.title: Preserve extension-specific config when re-importing existing projects
items.description:
Type: project config merge defect
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready (AFK)
Source: dedicated MCP-Pharo project setup on 2026-05-18.

Observed failure:
Re-importing an existing DevNexus-Pharo project reset `extensions["dev-nexus-pharo"]` from a configured object containing `plexusProjectConfig` and `imageExecution` to `{}`. The extension config then had to be restored manually.

Current evidence:
- `src/nexusProjectOperations.ts` merges project extensions with a shallow top-level spread: existing extension keys are retained, but a caller-provided entry for the same extension key replaces the whole nested object.
- DevNexus-Pharo `importDevNexusPharoProject` passes `extensions: devNexusPharoProjectExtensionEntry()`, whose default value is `{ "dev-nexus-pharo": {} }`.
- Existing tests cover retaining unrelated extension keys, but not preserving nested config for the same extension when the incoming value is an empty/default marker.

Goal:
Make re-import idempotent for extension-owned project settings while still allowing intentional extension config replacement when a caller explicitly requests it.

Acceptance criteria:
- Re-importing an initialized project with an empty/default extension marker preserves existing nested extension config for that extension key.
- Callers still have an explicit way to replace or clear extension config when that is intended.
- Tests cover same-key nested extension preservation, same-key intentional override, and unrelated extension retention.
- DevNexus-Pharo project import no longer clobbers `plexusProjectConfig` or `imageExecution` on existing projects.

Boundaries:
- Keep merge semantics generic in DevNexus core; do not hard-code DevNexus-Pharo keys in core.
- A DevNexus-Pharo-side change may still be needed if the plugin should avoid passing an empty extension marker on re-import.

Verification:
- Focused `nexusProjectOperations` import tests.
- DevNexus-Pharo import/idempotence test if plugin call behavior changes.
- `npm run check` in affected component(s) when feasible.

items.title: Resolve component docs in worker context bundles
items.description:
Type: DevNexus worktree setup defect
Source: DevNexus PLexus project plexus local-31 on 2026-05-18.

Finding: component worktree preparation can fail after creating the worktree when a work item mentions a component-local documentation file. The worker context builder treats the relative documentation path as project metadata context and validates it under the DevNexus project root, even when the file exists in the selected component checkout/worktree.

Goal: worker context bundles should include component documentation references from the selected component source root when the project metadata root does not contain the referenced file.

Acceptance criteria:
- Existing project-root planning document references keep resolving as project context.
- Component-local documentation references resolve against the selected component source root and appear as read-only component context.
- Missing documentation errors name both the project metadata root and component source root scopes that were checked.
- Regression coverage proves a component doc present only in the component source root does not fail worktree preparation.

items.title: Use component repository in coordination integration plans
items.description:
Type: DevNexus coordination tooling defect
Source: DevNexus PLexus project plexus local-30 on 2026-05-18.

Finding: `coordination_integrate` accepts `componentId: "plexus"` and returns the selected component metadata, but still computes `target.commit` and merge analysis from the DevNexus project metadata repository. In the PLexus repro, `target.commit` was `504fe72b7b1798e81cd16b55875995041ca8d864`, the project metadata `main`, while PLexus component `main` was `764edb5a45ed1fe21cc709f3228b1663025a6b05`.

Goal: coordination integration planning must analyze the selected component repository when `componentId` is supplied, including target branch lookup, handoff branch merge analysis, changed files, conflict files, range-diff, and missing-branch errors.

Acceptance criteria:
- When a git component is selected, target branch resolution uses the component source root/worktree repository, not the DevNexus project metadata root.
- Returned target commit and merge diagnostics are from the selected component repo.
- If a handoff branch is absent from the selected component repo, the warning/error names that component repo and branch.
- Regression coverage uses a project metadata repo and a component repo with distinct `main` commits to prove the component repo is used.

items.title: Repair setup assistant full-check failures blocking dogfood publication
items.description:
Type: DevNexus verification blocker
Source: DevNexus PLexus dogfood work on 2026-05-18.

Finding: `npm run check` fails on DevNexus branches that otherwise pass focused tests and build because `src/nexusSetupAssistant.test.ts` has 6 failing assertions. The failures are unrelated to the worker-context and coordination-integration patches, but they block direct publication according to the component verification policy.

Observed failures:
- macOS setup plan tests expect `$HOME/dev-nexus/...` clone guidance but commands no longer include it.
- Windows setup check tests create POSIX fixture paths, while current checks resolve component paths under `$env:USERPROFILE\dev-nexus\...`, causing missing-check and blocked-status results.
- Agent MCP/session/plugin projection tests expect warning overall status but receive blocked because component source root checks fail first.

Goal: either correct setup assistant behavior or update the tests to the intended platform/path semantics so full `npm run check` is meaningful again.

Acceptance criteria:
- `npm test -- src/nexusSetupAssistant.test.ts` passes.
- Full `npm run check` passes on a clean DevNexus worktree.
- The chosen behavior clearly distinguishes plan-time platform-specific guidance from check-time host filesystem validation.

items.title: Decide DevNexus-Research license and ARS integration posture
items.description:
Type: human-in-the-loop product and licensing decision
Readiness: blocked until the user/project owner decides the desired upstream integration posture.
Source PRD: docs/dev-nexus-research-plugin-prd.md
Inspiration source: Imbad0202/academic-research-skills and Imbad0202/academic-research-skills-codex.

Goal: decide how DevNexus-Research may use Academic Research Skills (ARS) material before any ARS-derived content is bundled, adapted, or redistributed.

Decision options:
- Inspiration only: use ARS as an attributed product reference and write DevNexus-Research content independently.
- Optional external integration: detect or document the user-installed ARS Codex academic-research-suite skill and project DevNexus context around it.
- Bundled/adapted content: vendor or adapt ARS material, requiring explicit acceptance of CC BY-NC 4.0 constraints or separate permission for broader/commercial redistribution.

Acceptance criteria:
- The chosen integration posture is recorded in target state or a follow-up Architecture Decision Record.
- Commercial/noncommercial expectations are explicit.
- Attribution requirements are documented for any ARS-inspired or ARS-derived material.
- Implementation slices state whether they may use ARS content directly or only reference it.
- No DevNexus-Research package publication depends on ambiguous license status.

Boundaries:
- Do not copy ARS prompts, agents, examples, schemas, or documentation into DevNexus source until this decision is resolved.
- Do not present DevNexus-Research as endorsed by ARS upstream.

Verification:
- Static review of docs and package metadata for license posture and attribution notes.

items.title: Scaffold DevNexus-Research plugin component
items.description:
Type: plugin scaffold tracer-bullet
Readiness: autonomous agent-ready if implemented with original DevNexus-owned content only.
Source PRD: docs/dev-nexus-research-plugin-prd.md
Related blocker: dev-nexus:local-146 blocks ARS-derived or bundled content, but not an original plugin skeleton.

Goal: create the initial DevNexus-Research plugin package or component skeleton that proves research can be modeled as an additive DevNexus plugin.

Acceptance criteria:
- A DevNexus-Research package/component exists with package metadata, README, tests, and a minimal exported plugin capability config.
- The plugin declares only generic DevNexus plugin capability records such as projected_skill, setup_obligation, environment_hint, worker_context_fragment, and worker_briefing_fragment.
- The skeleton uses original placeholder skills and guidance, with no copied ARS content.
- The plugin can be referenced from a DevNexus project config without adding research-specific behavior to DevNexus core.
- Documentation states that the plugin is a domain add-on and that DevNexus still owns generic project, tracker, worktree, and coordination behavior.

Boundaries:
- Do not vendor ARS or adapt ARS prompt text in this slice.
- Do not add live external API calls, document rendering, Zotero integration, or citation-provider clients.
- Do not add the plugin to the dogfood root as enabled until setup and projection behavior is tested.

Verification:
- Package build/test for the new component.
- Static project-config fixture proving the plugin capability shape validates.
- npm run check for the component when feasible.

items.title: Project baseline research workflow skills
items.description:
Type: projected skill implementation slice
Readiness: dependent on dev-nexus:local-147 plugin skeleton; autonomous if content is original and not ARS-derived.
Source PRD: docs/dev-nexus-research-plugin-prd.md

Goal: add DevNexus-Research projected skills for baseline academic workflows with progressive disclosure and human-in-the-loop guidance.

User stories covered:
- As a researcher with a broad topic, I can route to research-question scoping before drafting.
- As an author, I can request paper planning, revision, citation-integrity, or manuscript-review support without loading every workflow.
- As a coordinator, I can see which research skills are available in worker context.

Acceptance criteria:
- The plugin exposes a small router skill plus workflow-specific skills or references for research-question scoping, literature review, systematic-review protocol, paper planning, revision, manuscript review, citation integrity, research artifact handoff, and experiment planning.
- Skills use original DevNexus-Research text unless dev-nexus:local-146 explicitly permits ARS adaptation.
- Skill metadata names intended agent targets and supports active-target filtering.
- Worker context lists available research workflows without overwhelming unrelated workers.
- Review-oriented guidance is read-only unless the user explicitly enters a revision workflow.

Boundaries:
- Do not copy ARS prompt text or agent files while local-146 is unresolved.
- Do not create native slash commands or agent-provider-specific command registries in this slice.

Verification:
- Focused skill projection tests for active target filtering and worker context summaries.
- Static lint/check of projected skill metadata.
- Component check command when feasible.

items.title: Add research artifact conventions and setup checks
items.description:
Type: setup and artifact model slice
Readiness: dependent on dev-nexus:local-147 plugin skeleton.
Source PRD: docs/dev-nexus-research-plugin-prd.md

Goal: let DevNexus-Research projects declare and report research artifacts, optional tool readiness, and provenance-oriented setup state.

User stories covered:
- As a researcher, I can see expected research artifacts such as research brief, source manifest, material passport, claim register, review package, revision matrix, and export decision.
- As a coordinator, I can detect missing optional tools before assigning citation, document-export, or source-verification work.
- As a project owner, I can keep API keys and secret paths out of portable project state.

Acceptance criteria:
- The plugin defines configurable artifact conventions for research brief, source manifest, material passport, claim register, review package, revision matrix, and export decision.
- Setup checks report optional availability for bibliography/source tooling, document export tooling, PDF text extraction, and external index/API profiles without requiring them in baseline mode.
- Worker briefing explains which artifacts are expected, present, missing, generated, or user-owned.
- Environment hints carry path/profile names only and never secret values.
- Missing optional tools degrade into explicit setup warnings, not silent runtime failures.

Boundaries:
- Do not run live provider/API calls in default tests.
- Do not require Pandoc, LaTeX, Zotero, or network access for the baseline plugin.
- Do not impose fixed file paths on every research project.

Verification:
- Fixture tests for artifact path rendering and setup readiness output.
- Tests for no-network/no-optional-tool baseline behavior.
- Component check command when feasible.

items.title: Integrate optional external ARS Codex skill projection
items.description:
Type: optional integration slice
Readiness: dependent on dev-nexus:local-147 and the dev-nexus:local-146 license posture for any behavior beyond detection/documentation.
Source PRD: docs/dev-nexus-research-plugin-prd.md
Upstream candidate: Imbad0202/academic-research-skills-codex academic-research-suite skill.

Goal: let DevNexus-Research optionally detect or guide installation of the ARS Codex academic-research-suite skill and project DevNexus artifact/context hints around it.

Acceptance criteria:
- The plugin can report whether the external ARS Codex skill is installed or expected for the active agent target.
- Setup guidance explains how to install or update the external skill without vendoring it into DevNexus-Research.
- Worker context can reference the external skill only when configured and present, with attribution and license notes.
- Missing external ARS skill is an optional setup warning unless the research project explicitly requires it.
- The integration does not copy ARS content into DevNexus-Research source.

Boundaries:
- Do not make ARS a hard dependency for baseline DevNexus-Research.
- Do not invoke package installers automatically unless an explicit setup flow owns that action.
- Do not claim feature parity with ARS; this is an optional bridge.

Verification:
- Mocked installed/missing skill detection tests.
- Static docs checks for attribution and optionality.
- Component check command when feasible.

items.title: Add DevNexus-Research dogfood paper-project smoke
items.description:
Type: dogfood smoke slice
Readiness: dependent on dev-nexus:local-147, local-148, and local-149.
Source PRD: docs/dev-nexus-research-plugin-prd.md

Goal: create a minimal research-project fixture or dogfood example proving DevNexus can support non-engineering academic work without source-code assumptions.

Acceptance criteria:
- A minimal DevNexus research project can enable DevNexus-Research and render worker context without a software component verification command.
- The project can declare a research brief, source manifest, material passport, and output artifact paths.
- Setup status reports optional research tools as present, missing, or not configured without failing the baseline project.
- A smoke workflow demonstrates research-question scoping or artifact handoff using original placeholder materials.
- The smoke does not require network APIs, paid model APIs, Zotero, Pandoc, LaTeX, or a real unpublished paper.

Boundaries:
- Use a synthetic fixture or explicitly approved public/open research note; do not commit private paper materials.
- Do not publish or represent generated fixture text as real scholarship.
- Do not include ARS-derived content unless dev-nexus:local-146 permits it.

Verification:
- Fixture smoke command or test that renders project status/setup/worker context.
- git diff --check.
- Component check command when feasible.

local-1.body: Coordinator selected this item for the current dogfood cycle. Scope: use the to-issues skill to refine the seeded component-owned local work items with acceptance criteria, HITL/AFK classification, blockers, and verification notes.

Completed: all seeded local work items across DevNexus, DevNexus-Pharo, PLexus, pharo-launcher-mcp, and MCP-Pharo now have component-owned issue bodies with readiness classification, blockers, acceptance criteria, and safe verification notes.

local-2.body: Coordinator selected this item for source/docs hardening: expose the result-file contract in agent launch context and keep the launcher validation strict.

Completed in DevNexus commit 95cec72: agent launch context now includes a result contract with the result file path, required/optional fields, statuses, verification statuses, and publication decision types; environment variables expose concise required/optional field hints; focused and full checks passed.

local-3.body: Completed in DevNexus commit 1863d04. Target-report relaunch decisions now prefer the latest target cycle runId when present, CLI/MCP report tests assert the decision shape, and focused wait/failed decision coverage was added. Verification passed: npm test -- src/nexusAutomationTargetReport.test.ts src/cli.test.ts src/nexusMcpServer.test.ts; npm run check.

local-16.body: Still blocked on a shared-coordination decision. Recommended minimal unblock: use pushed Git branches plus GitHub-backed work items/handoffs as the durable shared source of truth; treat Tailscale MCP as optional live transport, not the only source of truth; keep locks out of the first rollout.

Coordination investigation update: user deprioritized Tailscale for now and wants other shared work-item sources. Recommended path to evaluate first: GitHub Issues or GitHub Projects in a dedicated coordination organization/repository, accessed by DevNexus through a least-privilege bot/GitHub App. Linear is attractive if using OpenAI Codex cloud task delegation directly; Codex itself does not appear to provide a first-party Kanban/work-item database for DevNexus to use as source of truth.

User direction update: do not prioritize Tailscale for shared work items now. Do not push DevNexus meta-project repos into Evref-BL. Investigate a dedicated private GitHub organization and coordination repository/project, with automation acting through a clearly identified bot identity rather than the user personal account.
